question: What is a hub?
choice_a: A Layer 2 device that forwards frames based on destination MAC addresses
choice_b: A Layer 3 device that routes traffic between different IP subnets
choice_c: A Layer 1 device that repeats incoming electrical signals out all other ports
choice_d: A device that manages VLAN assignments across multiple switches
explanation: A hub is a Layer 1 device. It simply repeats an incoming electrical signal out every other port without inspecting frame contents or caring about the intended destination.
aspect_key: hub repeats signals out all ports without inspecting frame contents
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the primary job of a switch?
choice_a: To repeat electrical signals out all ports to maximize coverage
choice_b: To translate IP addresses into MAC addresses for local delivery
choice_c: To encrypt traffic between VLANs for security
choice_d: To inspect Ethernet headers and forward frames based on destination MAC addresses
explanation: A switch is a Layer 2 device. It reads the destination MAC address in an Ethernet frame and forwards the frame only to the port associated with that MAC address.
aspect_key: switch inspects ethernet headers and forwards based on mac address
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: How long is a MAC address?
choice_a: 16 bits
choice_b: 32 bits
choice_c: 128 bits
choice_d: 48 bits
explanation: A MAC address is 48 bits long; typically written as six octets separated by hyphens (e.g. 21-76-3D-7A-F6-1E). Each octet is 8 bits so six octets = 48 bits total.
aspect_key: mac address is 48 bits written as six hyphen-separated octets
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: How does a switch learn which port a device is connected to?
choice_a: It queries a central DHCP server that maps MAC addresses to switch ports
choice_b: It reads the source MAC address on incoming frames and associates it with the port the frame arrived on
choice_c: It broadcasts a discovery packet and waits for devices to reply with their port numbers
choice_d: It uses ARP to resolve IP addresses to MAC addresses and stores the result in its routing table
explanation: The learning process: when a frame arrives the switch reads the source MAC address and records it along with the ingress port in the MAC address table. Future frames destined for that MAC are forwarded directly to the learned port.
aspect_key: switch learns source mac and ingress port from each incoming frame
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What does a switch do when the destination MAC address is not in its MAC address table?
choice_a: It drops the frame and sends an ICMP unreachable message to the sender
choice_b: It forwards the frame to the default gateway for resolution
choice_c: It sends the frame only to the port configured as the uplink
choice_d: It floods the frame out all ports except the port the frame arrived on
explanation: Flooding is the fallback behavior when the switch has no learned entry for the destination MAC. The switch sends the frame to all ports except the ingress port so the unknown destination has a chance to receive and respond.
aspect_key: unknown destination mac causes switch to flood out all ports except ingress
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is a VLAN?
choice_a: A physical cable standard for separating department networks at the wiring closet
choice_b: A Layer 3 routing protocol for connecting remote offices over the internet
choice_c: A logical Layer 2 network built on shared physical switch infrastructure that separates traffic without physical rewiring
choice_d: A wireless segmentation method for isolating Wi-Fi traffic from wired traffic
explanation: A VLAN (Virtual Local Area Network) is a logical grouping of switch ports that creates a separate Layer 2 broadcast domain. Devices in different VLANs are isolated at Layer 2 even though they share the same physical switch.
aspect_key: vlan is a logical layer 2 network on shared physical switch infrastructure
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 16 - VLANs Made Easy/Transcript.md

question: What is an access port?
choice_a: A port that carries traffic for multiple VLANs using 802.1Q tagging
choice_b: A port that connects a switch to a router for inter-VLAN routing
choice_c: A high-speed uplink port used for inter-switch connections
choice_d: A port that carries traffic for only one VLAN and sends frames without a VLAN tag
explanation: An access port connects an endpoint device and carries traffic for exactly one VLAN. Frames leaving an access port are untagged — the endpoint device receives a normal Ethernet frame with no VLAN identifier.
aspect_key: access port carries one vlan and delivers untagged frames to endpoints
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is a collision domain?
choice_a: The set of devices that receive a Layer 2 broadcast frame
choice_b: The IP subnet range shared by devices on the same network segment
choice_c: The group of switch ports that share the same VLAN assignment
choice_d: The segment of a network where simultaneous transmissions collide and corrupt each other
explanation: A collision domain is any network segment where two devices transmitting at the same time produce a collision. Hubs keep all ports in one large collision domain. Switches break each port link into its own collision domain.
aspect_key: collision domain is where simultaneous transmissions cause collisions
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is a broadcast domain?
choice_a: The area of the network where 802.1Q trunk links carry multiple VLANs simultaneously
choice_b: The set of switch ports that share the same collision detection mechanism
choice_c: The segment of a network where a Layer 2 broadcast frame is received by all devices
choice_d: The range of IP addresses assigned by a DHCP server to hosts on a subnet
explanation: A broadcast domain is the set of devices that all receive a broadcast frame (destination MAC FF-FF-FF-FF-FF-FF). Switches forward broadcasts to all ports in the same VLAN; routers and VLANs segment broadcast domains.
aspect_key: broadcast domain is where all devices receive a layer 2 broadcast frame
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What does VTP stand for and what is its purpose?
choice_a: Virtual Trunk Protocol — the actual tagging standard for VLAN frames on trunk links
choice_b: VLAN Transfer Protocol — a Cisco protocol for migrating VLAN configs between switches of different vendors
choice_c: VLAN Trunking Protocol — a Cisco protocol that replicates VLAN information between Cisco switches over trunk links
choice_d: Virtual Tagging Protocol — the IEEE standard for attaching VLAN IDs to Ethernet frames
explanation: VTP (VLAN Trunking Protocol) is Cisco proprietary. It is not the mechanism that tags frames; 802.1Q does that. VTP replicates VLAN database information (VLAN IDs and names) between Cisco switches so they do not all need to be configured manually.
aspect_key: vtp replicates vlan information between cisco switches over trunk links
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is trunking in a switched network?
choice_a: A method of encrypting traffic between two switches to prevent eavesdropping
choice_b: Allowing traffic for multiple VLANs to travel across one physical link using VLAN tagging
choice_c: A redundancy protocol that prevents loops on inter-switch links
choice_d: The process of aggregating multiple physical links into one logical link for increased bandwidth
explanation: Trunking lets one physical link carry traffic for multiple VLANs simultaneously. Each frame is tagged (802.1Q) so the receiving device knows which VLAN it belongs to. Without trunking VLANs could not extend across multiple switches.
aspect_key: trunking carries multiple vlans over one physical link using vlan tags
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md

question: What IEEE standard defines VLAN tagging on trunk links?
explanation: IEEE 802.1Q is the industry-standard method for tagging VLAN traffic on trunk links. It inserts a 4-byte tag into the Ethernet frame identifying the VLAN. All VLAN-aware devices on the trunk must understand 802.1Q to process tagged frames.
aspect_key: 802.1q is the ieee standard for vlan tagging on trunk links
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md

question: What is SSH and why is it used for network device management?
choice_a: SSH is a file sharing protocol used to move configuration files between network devices
choice_b: SSH is a Layer 2 protocol for encrypting VLAN traffic on trunk links
choice_c: SSH is a secure remote terminal protocol that encrypts credentials and commands; used to manage routers and switches over the network
choice_d: SSH is a monitoring protocol that collects interface statistics from managed switches
explanation: SSH (Secure Shell) provides encrypted remote CLI access to network devices. It replaces Telnet which sends all data — including passwords — in cleartext. SSH encrypts the entire session protecting credentials from eavesdropping.
aspect_key: ssh is an encrypted remote terminal protocol for managing network devices
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 10 - Telnet and SSH/Transcript.md

question: What is a router's primary function?
choice_a: To repeat electrical signals to extend the reach of an Ethernet segment
choice_b: To learn MAC addresses and forward frames within a Layer 2 network
choice_c: To forward traffic based on IP addresses connecting different IP networks or subnets
choice_d: To assign VLAN IDs to ports on a managed switch
explanation: A router is a Layer 3 device. It examines destination IP addresses in packets and forwards them toward the correct subnet. Routers connect separate IP networks — and are required to connect a LAN to the internet.
aspect_key: router forwards traffic based on ip addresses between different subnets
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is NAT?
choice_a: A protocol that converts MAC addresses to IP addresses for Layer 3 routing
choice_b: A method that lets multiple private devices share one public IP address at the router
choice_c: A VLAN tagging mechanism used by routers to separate broadcast domains
choice_d: A routing protocol used to exchange subnet information between routers
explanation: NAT (Network Address Translation) allows many private IP hosts to share one public IP. The router substitutes its public IP as the source when internal hosts reach the internet and tracks sessions to return replies to the correct internal host.
aspect_key: nat lets multiple private devices share one public ip at the router
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is DTP?
choice_a: Dynamic Trunking Protocol — a Cisco protocol that negotiates whether a switch link should become a trunk
choice_b: Dynamic Transfer Protocol — the mechanism that moves VLAN databases between switches
choice_c: Designated Trunk Protocol — the IEEE standard that replaced ISL for VLAN tagging
choice_d: Direct Trunk Path — the physical uplink between a distribution switch and a core switch
explanation: DTP (Dynamic Trunking Protocol) is Cisco proprietary. It negotiates trunk formation between Cisco switch ports. DTP auto mode waits passively; DTP desirable mode actively tries to form a trunk. Hard-coding port modes is preferred over relying on DTP.
aspect_key: dtp negotiates trunk formation between cisco switch ports
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is a trunk port?
choice_a: A port reserved for management traffic and switch configuration
choice_b: A port that connects only PoE-powered devices like IP phones and cameras
choice_c: A port that carries traffic for only one VLAN and delivers frames untagged
choice_d: A port that carries traffic for more than one VLAN using 802.1Q tagging
explanation: A trunk port carries multiple VLANs over one physical link. Each frame is tagged with an 802.1Q VLAN ID. Trunk ports commonly connect switches to other switches; to routers; or to VLAN-aware servers.
aspect_key: trunk port carries multiple vlans using 802.1q tagging
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md

question: What is a VPN?
choice_a: A VLAN protocol that uses encryption to protect inter-VLAN traffic on trunk links
choice_b: An encrypted connection built across a public network like the internet to create a secure private path
choice_c: A protocol for dynamically assigning IP addresses to remote access devices
choice_d: A Cisco proprietary trunking method replaced by 802.1Q
explanation: A VPN (Virtual Private Network) is an encrypted tunnel built over the internet. Common use cases are giving remote workers secure access to the company network (remote-access VPN) and connecting offices in different cities (site-to-site VPN).
aspect_key: vpn is an encrypted connection over a public network creating a secure private path
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What does a switch do with a frame that has destination MAC FF-FF-FF-FF-FF-FF?
choice_a: It drops the frame because FF-FF-FF-FF-FF-FF is not a valid unicast destination
choice_b: It forwards the frame only to the port listed in the MAC address table for that address
choice_c: It sends the frame to the default gateway for routing
choice_d: It forwards the frame to all ports except the ingress port as a broadcast
explanation: FF-FF-FF-FF-FF-FF is the Layer 2 broadcast address. When a switch receives a frame with this destination it forwards a copy out every port in the same VLAN except the port the frame came in on. All devices in the broadcast domain receive it.
aspect_key: ff-ff-ff-ff-ff-ff destination causes switch to broadcast to all ports
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the MAC address table also called?
choice_a: The IP routing table
choice_b: The ARP cache
choice_c: The forwarding database
choice_d: The VLAN assignment table
explanation: The MAC address table — which maps learned MAC addresses to switch ports — is also called the forwarding database. Both terms refer to the same data structure the switch uses to make frame forwarding decisions.
aspect_key: mac address table is also called the forwarding database
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What does Telnet send over the network?
choice_a: Only usernames in plaintext while passwords are encrypted with a one-way hash
choice_b: Usernames; passwords; commands; and all output in plaintext with no encryption
choice_c: Encrypted command output but plaintext usernames to identify the user
choice_d: Only configuration data encrypted; interactive session traffic is excluded
explanation: Telnet is a legacy protocol that transmits all session data — including usernames; passwords; and every command typed — in plaintext. Anyone monitoring the network path can capture and read the credentials directly.
aspect_key: telnet sends all credentials and commands in plaintext with no encryption
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 10 - Telnet and SSH/Transcript.md

question: What is a managed switch?
choice_a: A switch that forwards frames only at Layer 1 using signal amplification
choice_b: A switch with a fixed configuration that cannot be changed after manufacture
choice_c: A high-speed core switch designed only for inter-switch uplink connections
choice_d: A switch that can be configured and monitored remotely often via a web interface or CLI
explanation: A managed switch supports remote configuration and monitoring. It typically has an IP address for management access; supports VLAN configuration; and may offer a web interface or CLI (via Telnet or SSH). VLAN deployment requires managed switches.
aspect_key: managed switch supports remote configuration and monitoring
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What happens to the collision domain when a hub is replaced with a switch?
choice_a: The switch merges all device collision domains into a single shared segment
choice_b: The collision domain is unchanged because switches still share physical media
choice_c: Each switch port becomes its own separate collision domain eliminating most collision risk
choice_d: The switch eliminates all broadcast domains in addition to collision domains
explanation: Hubs put all connected devices into one large collision domain. A switch creates a separate collision domain per port link. This means two devices communicating through a switch do not interfere with other pairs communicating on other ports simultaneously.
aspect_key: switch creates separate collision domain per port eliminating shared collisions
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What Layer of the OSI model does a router operate at?
choice_a: Layer 1 — physical
choice_b: Layer 2 — data link
choice_c: Layer 4 — transport
choice_d: Layer 3 — network
explanation: Routers operate at Layer 3 (network layer) of the OSI model. They make forwarding decisions based on IP addresses. Switches primarily operate at Layer 2 (data link) using MAC addresses.
aspect_key: router operates at layer 3 network layer using ip addresses
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Why is traffic between two VLANs on the same switch not forwarded at Layer 2?
choice_a: Switches always drop traffic between VLANs to protect the MAC address table from being overwritten
choice_b: VLANs operate at Layer 3 so they require NAT translation before forwarding between segments
choice_c: Each VLAN is a separate Layer 2 broadcast domain and a switch only forwards frames within the same VLAN; a Layer 3 device is needed to route between VLANs
choice_d: Traffic between VLANs is denied by DTP until an administrator manually authorizes the path
explanation: VLANs create separate Layer 2 broadcast domains. A Layer 2 switch only forwards frames within the same VLAN. To move traffic from VLAN 10 to VLAN 20; a Layer 3 device (router or Layer 3 switch) must route the packet between subnets.
aspect_key: layer 2 switch cannot forward between vlans; a layer 3 device is required
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is a bridge?
choice_a: A Layer 2 device similar to a switch but typically with only two ports used to connect network segments
choice_b: A Layer 3 device that filters traffic by IP address between VLANs
choice_c: A physical cable connector used to join two fiber optic runs
choice_d: A software-only construct used to create virtual network interfaces inside a hypervisor
explanation: A bridge is a MAC-forwarding device like a switch but usually has only two ports. Bridges predate inexpensive multiport switches. A modern switch is essentially a multiport bridge. Bridges can also join different media types or speeds.
aspect_key: bridge is a mac-forwarding two-port device predecessor to modern switches
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is an SFP port?
choice_a: A software-defined port used to assign VLAN IDs on managed switches
choice_b: A modular high-speed interface port that accepts pluggable copper or fiber transceiver modules
choice_c: A dedicated management port used only for out-of-band switch administration
choice_d: A special port that supplies PoE power to connected devices
explanation: SFP (Small Form-Factor Pluggable) ports accept modular transceivers for different media types. You can insert an SFP module for 10 Gbps copper or 8 Gbps fiber depending on what the link requires. This gives the switch flexible high-speed connectivity options.
aspect_key: sfp is a modular pluggable interface port accepting copper or fiber transceivers
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What does router-on-a-stick mean?
choice_a: A router connected serially to a hub using a shared coaxial backbone cable
choice_b: A router that uses one physical interface with multiple subinterfaces over an 802.1Q trunk to route between VLANs
choice_c: A router that handles both NAT and VPN on the same physical device simultaneously
choice_d: A router with a single IP address shared across all VLANs for simplified management
explanation: Router-on-a-stick: one physical router port is trunked to the switch. The router creates subinterfaces (e.g. fa0/0.10 for VLAN 10; fa0/0.20 for VLAN 20) each with encapsulation dot1Q and a gateway IP. All inter-VLAN traffic routes through that one physical link.
aspect_key: router-on-a-stick uses one trunk interface with subinterfaces to route between vlans
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md

question: What problem does ARP use broadcasts to solve?
choice_a: ARP broadcasts ask routers which path to use to reach a remote subnet
choice_b: ARP broadcasts notify all hosts that a new device has joined the VLAN
choice_c: ARP broadcasts ask all devices on the local segment which MAC address corresponds to a given IP address
choice_d: ARP broadcasts advertise the default gateway IP to newly connected hosts
explanation: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses. When a host needs to send a frame to an IP address it does not have a MAC for; it broadcasts an ARP request to ask all local devices which one owns that IP. ARP is a common source of broadcast traffic.
aspect_key: arp broadcasts ask who owns a given ip address to get the corresponding mac
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the default VLAN that all switch ports belong to before any VLAN configuration is applied?
choice_a: VLAN 0
choice_b: VLAN 99
choice_c: VLAN 4094
choice_d: VLAN 1
explanation: On most switches all ports default to VLAN 1 out of the box. This means all devices connected to an unconfigured switch can communicate with each other. Administrators must explicitly assign ports to other VLANs to create logical separation.
aspect_key: all switch ports default to vlan 1 before any vlan configuration
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is an uplink port on a switch?
choice_a: A port that provides PoE power to connected IP phones and cameras
choice_b: A port that connects a switch to another switch carrying aggregated traffic from many downstream hosts
choice_c: A port that connects directly to end-user workstations and printers
choice_d: A port that provides console access for out-of-band management
explanation: An uplink is an inter-switch connection. Uplink ports carry aggregated traffic from all downstream hosts and are typically configured at higher speeds because they handle more traffic than ordinary access ports.
aspect_key: uplink is an inter-switch port carrying aggregated downstream traffic
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the security risk of leaving switch ports in DTP auto mode?
choice_a: Auto mode increases BPDU traffic causing STP to re-elect the root bridge more frequently
choice_b: Auto mode prevents VLAN tagging on the port making all traffic unreadable by other switches
choice_c: Auto mode enables attackers to negotiate a trunk connection gaining access to traffic for all VLANs
choice_d: Auto mode disables the port-security feature allowing any MAC address to connect
explanation: DTP auto mode waits passively for a trunk negotiation request. An unauthorized device that speaks DTP can trigger trunk formation allowing it to send and receive tagged traffic for all VLANs — a VLAN hopping risk. Hard-coding access mode prevents this.
aspect_key: auto mode allows unauthorized device to negotiate trunk and access all vlans
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: How does a router handle Layer 2 broadcasts?
choice_a: Routers amplify broadcasts and retransmit them to all connected subnets to ensure delivery
choice_b: Routers convert Layer 2 broadcasts into Layer 3 unicasts before routing them to the destination subnet
choice_c: Routers store Layer 2 broadcasts in the MAC table and forward them to the next hop
choice_d: Routers do not forward Layer 2 broadcasts; they block them at subnet boundaries
explanation: Routers are broadcast-boundary devices. A Layer 2 broadcast (FF-FF-FF-FF-FF-FF) does not cross a router interface. This is a key reason routers are used to connect separate subnets — each subnet has its own broadcast domain.
aspect_key: routers block layer 2 broadcasts segmenting broadcast domains at subnet boundaries
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is ISL in the context of VLAN trunking?
choice_a: ISL (Inter-Switch Link) is Cisco's older proprietary VLAN trunking method replaced by 802.1Q
choice_b: ISL (Internal Segment Link) is the IEEE standard for connecting switches at Layer 1
choice_c: ISL (Interface Speed Limiter) is a QoS mechanism that caps bandwidth on trunk ports
choice_d: ISL (Integrated Switch Layer) is a Cisco feature combining routing and switching in one device
explanation: ISL (Inter-Switch Link) was Cisco's proprietary VLAN trunking protocol. It encapsulated the entire Ethernet frame. ISL has been replaced by the vendor-neutral IEEE 802.1Q standard and is considered obsolete on modern networks.
aspect_key: isl is cisco proprietary vlan trunking replaced by 802.1q
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md

question: What is the difference between flooding and broadcasting on a switch?
choice_a: Flooding and broadcasting are the same operation; the terms are interchangeable
choice_b: Flooding sends a frame to all devices including devices in other VLANs; broadcasting stays within the local VLAN only
choice_c: Flooding is for unknown unicast destinations while broadcasting is intentional traffic addressed to all devices
choice_d: Flooding is a router function and broadcasting is a switch function
explanation: Flooding is what the switch does when the destination MAC is unknown — it sends the frame everywhere except the ingress port hoping to reach the destination. Broadcasting is intentional traffic using the FF-FF-FF-FF-FF-FF address meant for all devices on the segment.
aspect_key: flooding handles unknown unicast; broadcasting is intentional all-device traffic
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the VTP limitation administrators must understand?
choice_a: VTP can accidentally delete all VLANs if a switch with a higher revision number is introduced
choice_b: VTP assigns access ports to VLANs automatically making manual port assignment unnecessary
choice_c: VTP replicates VLAN information but does not assign switch ports to VLANs for you
choice_d: VTP only works over ISL trunks and is incompatible with 802.1Q
explanation: VTP reduces the need to manually create VLAN IDs and names on every switch by replicating the VLAN database. However VTP does not do port assignment — the administrator must still manually assign each access port to the correct VLAN on each switch.
aspect_key: vtp replicates vlan info but does not assign access ports to vlans
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What does it mean that a switch forwards frames transparently?
choice_a: The switch encrypts frames using a transparent cipher before sending them to the destination
choice_b: The switch strips and replaces headers with its own MAC address making itself invisible to endpoints
choice_c: The switch forwards an unchanged copy of the frame — endpoints cannot tell from the frame that a switch handled it
choice_d: The switch copies frames to a monitoring port while forwarding the original unchanged
explanation: Transparency means the switch does not alter the frame content during normal Layer 2 forwarding. The original source and destination MAC addresses are unchanged. The endpoint receiving the frame has no indication a switch was in the path.
aspect_key: switch forwards unchanged frame copy so endpoints cannot detect switch in path
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is a site-to-site VPN used for?
choice_a: Giving individual remote workers encrypted access to the company network from home
choice_b: Encrypting VLAN traffic between two switches in the same building
choice_c: Securely connecting two offices in different geographic locations over the internet
choice_d: Providing encrypted console access to Cisco routers in the same data center
explanation: A site-to-site VPN connects two office locations over the internet using an encrypted tunnel. Traffic between the offices appears to travel through a secure private link even though the underlying path crosses the public internet.
aspect_key: site-to-site vpn securely connects two offices over the internet
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Why is shared Ethernet a security problem?
choice_a: Shared Ethernet uses weak encryption that attackers can break with commodity hardware
choice_b: Shared Ethernet sends all traffic to all devices on the segment so any device can capture traffic meant for others
choice_c: Shared Ethernet requires plaintext IP addresses making ARP spoofing easier
choice_d: Shared Ethernet has no MAC address learning so every frame is flooded permanently
explanation: On a shared Ethernet segment (hub-based) every frame is delivered to every connected device. Each device is supposed to keep only frames addressed to itself — but nothing prevents a device in promiscuous mode from capturing all traffic. Privacy is not enforced by the hardware.
aspect_key: shared ethernet delivers all frames to all devices enabling passive traffic capture
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What two SSH authentication methods are supported?
choice_a: Certificate-based and token-based authentication
choice_b: RADIUS and TACACS+ server-based authentication
choice_c: Biometric and smart-card authentication
choice_d: Password authentication and public key authentication
explanation: SSH supports two main authentication methods: password authentication (username and password verified by the device) and public key authentication (the client has a private key and the device has the matching public key — no password transmitted).
aspect_key: ssh supports password authentication and public key authentication
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 10 - Telnet and SSH/Transcript.md

question: Why do VLANs avoid the need for physical redesign when reorganizing departments?
choice_a: VLANs use NAT to remap IP addresses so devices do not need to move
choice_b: VLANs allow departments to share one switch with logical isolation so ports are reassigned in software not by moving cables
choice_c: VLANs eliminate the need for a router so network changes require less physical infrastructure
choice_d: VLANs automatically detect device moves and update VLAN assignment without administrator intervention
explanation: Because VLAN membership is assigned by port in software an administrator can reassign a switch port to a different VLAN without touching any cables. This makes department moves and reorganizations much faster and cheaper than physically relocating hardware or rewiring.
concept_key: why vlans are easier than physical redesign
aspect_key: vlan port reassignment in software avoids physical cable moves during reorganization
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 16 - VLANs Made Easy/Transcript.md

question: What does it mean for a switch to buffer frames?
choice_a: A buffer is a VLAN assignment table that maps ports to VLANs temporarily
choice_b: A buffer is a collision recovery timer that prevents simultaneous transmissions
choice_c: Switches use memory buffers to hold a complete frame temporarily if the destination port is busy before forwarding it
choice_d: Buffering means the switch copies frames to a monitoring server before forwarding them
explanation: Switch buffers are memory areas that hold incoming frames while the destination port is occupied. Without buffers frames arriving faster than the destination port can transmit them would be dropped. Buffers allow the switch to absorb short bursts of traffic.
aspect_key: switch buffers hold frames in memory if destination port is busy
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What happens on a shared Ethernet segment when two devices transmit at the same time?
choice_a: The switch automatically retransmits both frames after a brief delay
choice_b: Both transmissions succeed because Ethernet uses full-duplex communication
choice_c: Both transmissions become corrupted in a collision and must be retransmitted after a random wait
choice_d: The hub selects the higher-priority transmission and drops the other
explanation: On shared media (hub or shared segment) simultaneous transmissions collide. The electrical signals mix and corrupt both frames. CSMA/CD detects the collision and both devices wait a random back-off time before retransmitting.
aspect_key: simultaneous transmissions on shared media cause collisions corrupting both frames
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Which of the following devices separates broadcast domains?
choice_a: Hub
choice_b: Access point
choice_c: Switch
choice_d: Router
explanation: Routers separate broadcast domains. A Layer 2 switch forwards broadcasts to all ports in the same VLAN — the entire switched network can still be one broadcast domain unless VLANs or routers segment it. Routers do not forward Layer 2 broadcasts across interfaces.
aspect_key: routers separate broadcast domains by blocking layer 2 broadcasts
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the best practice for configuring switch port modes to prevent VLAN hopping?
choice_a: Leave all ports in DTP auto mode so the switch can adapt dynamically to connected devices
choice_b: Use DTP desirable mode on all ports so they actively negotiate the best mode
choice_c: Configure all ports in DTP desirable so they form trunks with all connected equipment
choice_d: Explicitly configure access ports as access and trunk ports as trunk rather than relying on automatic negotiation
explanation: Relying on DTP auto-negotiation leaves ports open to unauthorized trunk formation. Best practice: hard-code every port — switchport mode access for endpoint ports and switchport mode trunk for inter-switch links. This eliminates ambiguity and prevents VLAN hopping via rogue trunk negotiation.
aspect_key: explicitly set access or trunk mode on every port instead of relying on dtp
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the VLAN hopping attack?
choice_a: An attack where a device spoofs a router MAC address to intercept inter-VLAN routed traffic
choice_b: An attack where a device gains unauthorized access to multiple VLANs by abusing trunk negotiation behavior
choice_c: An attack that floods the switch MAC table to force it into hub mode sending all traffic everywhere
choice_d: An attack that exhausts the PoE power budget of a switch by connecting many high-power devices
explanation: VLAN hopping occurs when an attacker connects a device that negotiates a trunk link (via DTP or double-tagging). Once trunked the attacker receives tagged frames for all VLANs not just their own. Hard-coding access mode and changing the native VLAN mitigate this.
aspect_key: vlan hopping gains access to multiple vlans by abusing trunk negotiation
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Why does a VLAN keep its broadcast traffic isolated from other VLANs?
choice_a: Because the switch uses NAT to rewrite broadcast addresses before crossing VLAN boundaries
choice_b: Because broadcasts use a destination IP that routers filter at each interface
choice_c: Because each VLAN is its own separate Layer 2 broadcast domain and switches only forward broadcasts within the same VLAN
choice_d: Because VLANs encrypt broadcast frames so other VLANs cannot decode them
explanation: A VLAN is a broadcast domain. When a device in VLAN 10 sends a broadcast the switch forwards it only to other ports in VLAN 10. Ports in VLAN 20 or other VLANs do not receive it. This is the core Layer 2 isolation mechanism that VLANs provide.
aspect_key: vlan is its own broadcast domain so broadcasts stay within the vlan only
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 16 - VLANs Made Easy/Transcript.md

question: What does NAT track to ensure internet replies reach the correct internal host?
choice_a: NAT tracks the VLAN ID of the original request to route replies to the correct segment
choice_b: NAT tracks the destination IP address of the server to forward replies to the correct subnet
choice_c: NAT tracks which internal host originated each session so it can map the incoming reply back to the correct private IP
choice_d: NAT tracks ARP entries and uses them to forward reply traffic to the requesting host
explanation: When an internal host sends traffic through NAT the router records the session mapping (internal IP and port; external IP and port; and translated public IP and port). When the reply arrives the router looks up this session record to restore the original private destination IP.
aspect_key: nat tracks original session to map internet replies back to the correct private host
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the same-cabling advantage of VLANs?
choice_a: VLANs use thinner cables than physical network segments allowing more devices per conduit
choice_b: VLANs allow multiple departments to share the same physical switch and cabling while keeping their traffic logically separated
choice_c: VLANs eliminate the need for patch cables by using wireless bridging within the switch chassis
choice_d: VLANs use the same cable for both power and data delivery eliminating the need for separate PoE cables
explanation: Without VLANs separating departments means separate physical switches and separate cable runs. VLANs let departments share the same switch hardware and cabling infrastructure while maintaining logical separation through port VLAN assignment.
aspect_key: vlans let departments share cabling and switches while remaining logically separated
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 16 - VLANs Made Easy/Transcript.md

question: What is a remote-access VPN used for?
choice_a: Connecting two offices in different cities through the internet with encrypted tunnels
choice_b: Providing external vendors with unencrypted access to network monitoring dashboards
choice_c: Giving remote workers encrypted secure access to the company network from an external location
choice_d: Extending the corporate VLAN to branch switches using 802.1Q over a WAN link
explanation: A remote-access VPN lets an employee working from home or traveling connect securely to the corporate network over the internet. The VPN client on their device creates an encrypted tunnel to the VPN concentrator giving access to internal resources.
aspect_key: remote-access vpn gives remote workers encrypted access to the company network
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: A hub connects 12 devices. How many collision domains exist and how would replacing it with a switch change this?
choice_a: 12 collision domains on the hub; the switch would merge them into 1
choice_b: 1 collision domain on the hub; the switch would create 1 collision domain per active port
choice_c: 1 collision domain on the hub; the switch would create 1 large shared collision domain for all ports
choice_d: All 12 devices have their own collision domain on the hub; the switch would reduce this to 1
explanation: A hub puts all ports in one collision domain — all 12 devices compete for the same medium. A switch creates a separate collision domain per active port link — 12 ports means 12 collision domains. Devices communicating on different pairs of ports do not interfere.
aspect_key: hub creates one collision domain; switch creates one per port
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Why does MAC address learning cause flooding to diminish over time on a switch?
choice_a: Flooding decreases because the switch eventually disables unused ports reducing the number of destinations
choice_b: Flooding decreases because the switch upgrades from Layer 2 to Layer 3 forwarding after building the MAC table
choice_c: Flooding decreases because as hosts communicate the switch learns their MAC-to-port mappings; subsequent traffic to known MACs is forwarded directly without flooding
choice_d: Flooding is replaced by ARP which provides MAC addresses so the switch never needs to flood again
explanation: Flooding is temporary. The first time an unknown unicast frame arrives the switch floods it. When the destination host responds the switch learns its MAC and port. From that point the switch forwards directly without flooding. Most entries are learned quickly as hosts communicate.
aspect_key: mac learning from replies eliminates flooding for known destinations over time
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Why would an uplink port between distribution and core switches need higher speed than access ports connecting workstations?
choice_a: Workstations negotiate to lower speeds automatically while uplinks stay at maximum speed by default
choice_b: Each uplink aggregates the combined traffic from all downstream hosts so it carries far more traffic than any single workstation link
choice_c: Uplinks require higher speed because 802.1Q tagging adds overhead that slows frame processing at standard speeds
choice_d: Uplinks must outrun ARP broadcast traffic which overwhelms standard-speed access ports
explanation: Access ports each carry one workstation's traffic. The uplink between switches carries the aggregate of all downstream hosts communicating upward simultaneously. A slow uplink creates a bottleneck for everyone on the segment. High-speed SFP ports or multi-gig links are used for uplinks and server connections for this reason.
aspect_key: uplinks carry aggregated traffic from all downstream hosts requiring higher speed
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: How does VTP reduce administrative burden in a multi-switch network?
choice_a: VTP automatically assigns ports to VLANs based on the device MAC address eliminating manual port assignment
choice_b: VTP eliminates the need for 802.1Q trunking by encrypting VLAN IDs into the standard Ethernet frame
choice_c: VTP uses the revision number system so the switch with the highest revision always pushes the authoritative VLAN database to all others; reducing per-switch manual configuration
choice_d: VTP replicates VLAN IDs and names from a VTP server to all VTP client switches so administrators create VLANs once on the server rather than manually on every switch
explanation: Without VTP administrators must create the same VLAN IDs and names on every switch individually. VTP lets one switch act as the server: create a VLAN there and it propagates to all VTP client switches over trunk links automatically.
aspect_key: vtp replicates vlan database from server to all clients reducing per-switch config
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What does 802.1Q conceptually do to a frame as it crosses a trunk link?
choice_a: 802.1Q encapsulates the entire Ethernet frame in a new outer header adding 26 bytes of overhead
choice_b: 802.1Q replaces the source MAC address with the VLAN ID so the destination switch knows which VLAN the frame belongs to
choice_c: 802.1Q inserts a VLAN tag into the frame so the receiving device knows which VLAN the frame belongs to
choice_d: 802.1Q compresses the payload of the frame to reduce trunk link bandwidth usage
explanation: As a frame leaves a VLAN-aware device on a trunk the switch inserts a 4-byte 802.1Q tag into the Ethernet frame. The tag contains the VLAN ID. The receiving device reads the tag; processes the frame in the correct VLAN; and strips the tag before forwarding to an access port.
aspect_key: 802.1q inserts a vlan tag into the frame so the receiving device identifies the vlan
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md

question: What is the difference between DTP auto mode and DTP desirable mode?
choice_a: Auto mode forms trunks with any device while desirable mode only forms trunks with matching Cisco equipment
choice_b: Auto and desirable behave identically; the terms only differ for documentation purposes
choice_c: Auto waits passively and does not initiate trunk formation; desirable actively sends DTP negotiation frames to form a trunk
choice_d: Auto mode requires 802.1Q while desirable mode uses ISL; the modes define which encapsulation is used
explanation: DTP auto: the port will become a trunk only if the other side actively initiates. DTP desirable: the port actively sends DTP frames trying to negotiate a trunk. Auto + Auto = no trunk (neither side initiates). Desirable + Auto or Desirable + Desirable = trunk forms.
aspect_key: auto waits passively for trunk; desirable actively initiates trunk negotiation
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Why does a switch need to know the destination MAC address is already in the MAC table before it can forward a frame directly?
choice_a: Without a MAC table entry the switch has no information about which port leads to the destination so it must flood to avoid dropping the frame
choice_b: The switch must confirm the MAC table entry is current by querying the DHCP server before forwarding
choice_c: MAC table lookup is only used to prevent collisions; forwarding decisions are still made by flooding
choice_d: Frames can only be forwarded directly if the destination port is in full-duplex mode regardless of MAC table state
explanation: If the MAC table has no entry for the destination MAC the switch cannot identify the correct egress port. Dropping the frame would be incorrect because the host may exist on a port that has not yet transmitted. Flooding ensures the frame reaches its destination even without a table entry.
aspect_key: mac table entry required to forward directly; missing entry triggers flooding
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the practical benefit of VLAN-to-subnet alignment in network design?
choice_a: VLANs that match subnets allow switches to route packets between them without a router
choice_b: VLAN-to-subnet alignment allows the switch to assign IP addresses automatically without DHCP
choice_c: Each VLAN functions as its own broadcast domain; aligning VLANs with IP subnets ensures the IP and Layer 2 boundaries match so routing between VLANs matches routing between subnets
choice_d: VLAN-to-subnet alignment enables ISL trunking to carry IP routing table information between switches
explanation: When each VLAN maps to one IP subnet the network architecture is clean: all hosts in a VLAN share a subnet; inter-VLAN traffic is inter-subnet traffic routed by a router or Layer 3 switch. Misalignment (multiple subnets per VLAN or multiple VLANs per subnet) causes operational and troubleshooting complexity.
aspect_key: aligning vlans with subnets makes layer 2 and layer 3 boundaries match
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: How does NAT allow a private internal network to communicate with the internet when private IP addresses are not routable on the public internet?
choice_a: NAT converts private IP addresses to IPv6 addresses which are routable globally
choice_b: NAT assigns a separate public IP address to every internal host from a large pool of public IPs
choice_c: NAT at the router substitutes its own public IP as the source address when internal packets leave and reverses the substitution when replies return
choice_d: NAT encrypts private IP addresses into a VPN header that internet routers can forward without knowing the internal addressing scheme
explanation: Private IP ranges (10.x; 172.16-31.x; 192.168.x) are not routable on the internet. When an internal host sends a packet outbound NAT replaces the private source IP with the router's public IP. The router tracks the session and when the reply comes back it substitutes the original private IP and forwards inward.
aspect_key: nat replaces private source ip with public router ip enabling internet communication
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the VLAN hopping threat model and what makes DTP auto mode the enabling vulnerability?
choice_a: VLAN hopping exploits STP to block ports selectively; DTP auto mode creates loop conditions that allow reachability across VLANs
choice_b: DTP auto mode causes ARP floods that allow attackers to intercept inter-VLAN routed traffic
choice_c: An attacker connects a device that speaks DTP; auto mode allows the port to negotiate to trunk mode giving the attacker tagged access to all VLANs — disabled by hard-coding access mode
choice_d: DTP auto mode encrypts traffic weakly; attackers decode the encryption to access any VLAN's traffic without needing trunk access
explanation: VLAN hopping threat: attacker plugs in a device that sends DTP negotiate frames. The switch port in auto mode accepts and forms a trunk. The attacker can now send and receive 802.1Q-tagged frames for any VLAN on that trunk. Hard-coding switchport mode access disables DTP completely on that port.
aspect_key: dtp auto mode enables vlan hopping by allowing attackers to negotiate trunk access
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Why does MAC-based forwarding still matter on a routed network even though routers use IP addresses?
choice_a: Once a router makes the IP routing decision the packet is still encapsulated in an Ethernet frame and local delivery on each segment depends on MAC addresses to reach the next hop
choice_b: MAC addresses are only relevant inside the router CPU for looking up the routing table; the Ethernet frame is discarded before the routing decision
choice_c: Routed networks replace MAC addresses with IP addresses at every hop so MAC is irrelevant beyond the first switch
choice_d: MAC addresses are used by the router to encrypt packet payloads before forwarding them between subnets
explanation: Routing decides which interface and next-hop to use (IP decision). But the actual frame sent on the local Ethernet segment must have the next-hop's MAC as the destination. ARP resolves the next-hop IP to a MAC. This Layer 2 delivery happens at every hop along the routed path.
concept_key: mac still matters in routed networks
aspect_key: local ethernet delivery still uses mac addresses even when routing by ip
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What are the three core switch behaviors described in Chapter 3 and what triggers each one?
choice_a: Routing; bridging; and switching — triggered by IP address lookup; MAC address lookup; and VLAN ID respectively
choice_b: Encrypting; decrypting; and forwarding — triggered by VPN negotiation; key exchange; and session establishment
choice_c: Learning; forwarding; and flooding — triggered by reading source MAC; destination MAC in table; and destination MAC unknown
choice_d: Trunking; tagging; and forwarding — triggered by DTP negotiation; 802.1Q insertion; and VLAN lookup
explanation: Three core switch behaviors: (1) Learning — triggered by any incoming frame; switch reads source MAC and ingress port into MAC table; (2) Forwarding — triggered when destination MAC is in the table; switch sends frame to the learned port only; (3) Flooding — triggered when destination MAC is unknown; switch sends to all ports except ingress.
aspect_key: learning from source mac; forwarding to known destination; flooding for unknown
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Why does connecting two switches together via trunks extend the broadcast domain across both switches?
choice_a: Trunking creates a Layer 3 path between switches causing routed broadcasts to propagate
choice_b: Trunking merges the MAC tables of both switches into one shared forwarding database
choice_c: Switches forward broadcasts to all ports in the same VLAN; with a trunk link frames tagged for a VLAN cross from one switch to the other so both switches' ports in that VLAN are in the same broadcast domain
choice_d: Trunking causes both switches to replicate all broadcast frames to every attached VLAN regardless of port assignment
explanation: Without a router or VLAN segmentation broadcast frames flood through trunk links. A broadcast from Host A on SW1 enters VLAN 1; the switch floods it out all VLAN 1 ports including the trunk to SW2; SW2 floods it out all its VLAN 1 ports. All hosts in VLAN 1 across both switches receive it.
aspect_key: trunk links propagate broadcasts across switches extending the broadcast domain
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: A virtualization server with multiple VMs needs each VM on a different VLAN. What switch configuration allows this on one physical NIC?
choice_a: Configure each VM as a separate physical device and connect each to its own switch access port using virtual cables
choice_b: Enable STP on the server's NIC so spanning tree can dynamically assign VLANs to each VM
choice_c: Configure a trunk link between the switch and the server's NIC; the hypervisor uses 802.1Q tagging to assign each VM to its VLAN
choice_d: Configure the server NIC as an access port in one VLAN and use NAT on the server to translate between VM subnets
explanation: A virtualization server NIC on a trunk link receives 802.1Q-tagged frames. The hypervisor (VMware; Hyper-V; etc.) reads the VLAN tag and forwards the frame to the correct virtual machine. Each VM belongs to a VLAN without needing a separate physical NIC.
aspect_key: trunk to server lets hypervisor use 802.1q tags to place each vm in a vlan
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md

question: How does a switch handle a frame with a known destination MAC that is reachable through a port leading to another switch?
choice_a: The switch drops the frame because only direct access-port destinations are supported
choice_b: The switch looks up the destination MAC in its MAC table; finds it associated with the uplink port leading to the other switch; and forwards the frame only out that port
choice_c: The switch floods the frame because it cannot confirm the destination is reachable through the uplink without an ARP reply
choice_d: The switch creates a new trunk session to the other switch before forwarding the frame
explanation: The MAC table maps destination MACs to egress ports. If the destination host is behind another switch the MAC is associated with the uplink port. The switch forwards the frame only out that port and it is the job of the next switch to deliver it further.
aspect_key: switch forwards to uplink port when destination mac is reachable through another switch
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the functional difference between the VTP server and VTP client roles?
choice_a: The VTP server creates the VLAN database and the VTP client provides Layer 3 routing for inter-VLAN traffic
choice_b: The VTP server runs one spanning-tree instance per VLAN while the VTP client runs one instance for all VLANs combined
choice_c: VTP server and client have no functional difference; the terms only describe which switch was configured first
choice_d: The VTP server creates and modifies the VLAN database and advertises it to VTP clients; VTP clients accept updates and store them but cannot make changes to the VLAN database
explanation: VTP server: administrator creates; modifies; and deletes VLANs here. Changes are advertised to all VTP clients in the same domain. VTP client: receives and applies VLAN database updates but cannot independently create or modify VLANs.
aspect_key: vtp server creates vlans and advertises to clients who cannot modify the database
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: A host in VLAN 30 needs to reach the internet. Describe the Layer 2 and Layer 3 handoff that must occur.
choice_a: The host sends a Layer 2 frame directly to the internet server's MAC address and the switch routes it to the internet based on VLAN ID
choice_b: The switch routes the packet based on the destination IP from VLAN 30 to the internet without a router
choice_c: The host sends an ARP for the destination IP; the switch responds; and the frame is forwarded directly across VLANs without routing
choice_d: The host sends traffic with the router's gateway IP as destination IP; the switch forwards it as Layer 2 within VLAN 30 to the router interface; the router strips Layer 2 and routes the IP packet toward the internet
explanation: Process: (1) host builds an IP packet to the internet destination; (2) host ARP-resolves the default gateway IP to get the router's MAC; (3) host sends an Ethernet frame with router MAC as destination within VLAN 30; (4) switch forwards it within VLAN 30 to the router port; (5) router strips L2; makes IP routing decision; sends outbound; NAT rewrites source IP.
aspect_key: host uses gateway mac to send frame to router within vlan then router routes ip packet
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the VLAN hopping security risk unique to the native VLAN when trunk negotiation is exploited?
choice_a: The native VLAN carries BPDUs which an attacker can intercept to modify STP topology
choice_b: Native VLAN frames are untagged on trunk links; an attacker can inject untagged frames that a switch forwards into the native VLAN bypassing VLAN access controls
choice_c: The native VLAN is always VLAN 1 which is also the management VLAN; hopping gives the attacker switch management access automatically
choice_d: The native VLAN frames are encrypted with a weaker cipher making them easier to intercept
explanation: 802.1Q native VLAN traffic is sent untagged on trunk links. If the attacker sends an untagged frame from a port in the same native VLAN it enters that VLAN without any explicit tagging. If native VLANs are mismatched an attacker can also use double-tagging to reach another VLAN. Best practice: change the native VLAN to an unused VLAN.
aspect_key: native vlan untagged frames can be injected bypassing vlan access controls
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: A network has grown large enough that ARP broadcasts and other overhead are significantly slowing performance. What is the most scalable solution?
choice_a: Replace all switches with hubs to reduce broadcast storms by eliminating the MAC table
choice_b: Add more routers and set every device to a static ARP entry to eliminate broadcasts
choice_c: Segment the network with VLANs and routers to create smaller broadcast domains reducing the number of devices each broadcast reaches
choice_d: Upgrade all switch ports to 10 Gbps so broadcasts consume a smaller percentage of available bandwidth
explanation: Each VLAN is its own broadcast domain. Splitting a flat network into multiple VLANs means ARP and other broadcasts only reach the devices within that VLAN. The total broadcast overhead each device processes drops significantly. Routers connect VLANs for inter-VLAN communication.
aspect_key: vlans reduce broadcast domains so each device processes fewer broadcasts
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 16 - VLANs Made Easy/Transcript.md

question: How does a bridge perform media conversion and what modern network role does it fill?
choice_a: Bridges convert between IPv4 and IPv6 address formats to connect legacy and modern subnets
choice_b: Bridges regenerate electrical signals between two cable segments; modern switches still use this for PoE delivery
choice_c: Bridges can connect different media types (copper on one side; fiber on the other) forwarding frames by MAC; modern switches are essentially multiport bridges fulfilling this same function
choice_d: Bridges translate between 802.1Q and ISL encapsulation; modern switches still perform this translation for legacy devices
explanation: A bridge connects different network media types (e.g. copper to fiber) and makes forwarding decisions based on MAC addresses. Modern multiport switches are the direct evolution of bridges — they do the same MAC-based forwarding with many more ports and higher speed.
aspect_key: bridges connect different media by mac forwarding; switches are multiport bridges
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Why does adding more devices to a hub-based shared Ethernet segment degrade performance non-linearly?
choice_a: Every device added increases collisions exponentially because the probability of two devices transmitting simultaneously rises with each additional sender competing for the same medium
choice_b: Hub performance degrades linearly; each additional device reduces total throughput by exactly one share of total bandwidth
choice_c: More devices cause the hub to enable rate limiting on all ports to prevent overload
choice_d: Hub performance degrades because each device added causes the hub to add a processing delay proportional to port count
explanation: Collision probability grows with the number of competing transmitters. With two devices collisions are rare. With many devices collisions are frequent; after each collision all parties back off and retry; causing more collisions in a feedback loop. This is why hubs scaled badly and why switches replaced them.
aspect_key: more hub devices increase collision probability and cause non-linear performance degradation
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the difference between a remote-access VPN and a site-to-site VPN in terms of endpoints and use case?
choice_a: Remote-access VPN connects two switches at different sites; site-to-site VPN connects a single user device to the corporate network
choice_b: Both VPN types are identical in topology; they differ only in the encryption protocol used
choice_c: Remote-access VPN connects an individual device to the corporate network for a mobile or home worker; site-to-site VPN persistently connects two entire office networks through an encrypted tunnel
choice_d: Site-to-site VPN is for temporary access during meetings; remote-access VPN is a permanent inter-office connection
explanation: Remote-access VPN: a single user's device (laptop; phone) connects to the corporate VPN concentrator. Useful for traveling employees or remote workers. Site-to-site VPN: a router at each office builds a persistent encrypted tunnel so all devices at both sites can reach each other as if on the same network.
aspect_key: remote-access connects one device to corporate; site-to-site connects two office networks
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: A switch has the following MAC table: port 1 = A1; port 2 = B2; port 3 = C3. A frame arrives on port 1 with source A1 and destination D4 which is unknown. What does the switch do?
choice_a: Update the MAC table with D4 on port 1 and send the frame to port 1
choice_b: Drop the frame because D4 is not in the MAC table and flooding is disabled by default
choice_c: Flood the frame out ports 2 and 3 only and learn A1 on port 1
choice_d: Forward the frame only to port 3 because C3 is the closest alphabetical match to D4
explanation: The switch processes the incoming frame: (1) Learning — source A1 is already known on port 1; no update needed; (2) Forwarding decision — D4 is not in the MAC table; (3) Flooding — switch floods out all ports except ingress port 1; so the frame goes to ports 2 and 3.
aspect_key: unknown destination mac triggers flooding out all ports except ingress
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the security argument for using SSH instead of Telnet even when managing a device on an internal trusted network?
choice_a: SSH is required by Cisco IOS and Telnet is disabled by default so there is no practical choice
choice_b: SSH provides authentication while Telnet does not authenticate the user at all
choice_c: Internal networks still carry traffic that can be captured by compromised hosts; SSH encrypts credentials protecting against insider threats and lateral movement by attackers who have already breached the network
choice_d: Telnet passwords are too short (4 characters max) while SSH enforces longer passwords protecting brute-force attacks
explanation: Even on a trusted internal network a compromised host or network tap can capture Telnet credentials. SSH encrypts the entire session so a captured packet reveals nothing useful. This is especially important for switch and router management where captured credentials could give an attacker full network control.
aspect_key: ssh encrypts credentials protecting against internal sniffing and compromised hosts
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 10 - Telnet and SSH/Transcript.md

question: What determines whether a DTP negotiation between two Cisco switch ports results in a trunk?
choice_a: The port with the higher bridge priority becomes the trunk initiator; the lower priority port accepts
choice_b: Trunk formation depends on the VLAN IDs configured on each port; matching VLANs always form a trunk
choice_c: If either port is in desirable mode or both are in desirable mode a trunk forms; if both are in auto mode no trunk forms because neither side initiates
choice_d: Trunk formation requires manual confirmation from the administrator regardless of DTP mode
explanation: DTP outcomes: Auto + Auto = no trunk (neither side sends DTP negotiate). Desirable + Auto = trunk (desirable sends; auto accepts). Desirable + Desirable = trunk (both send; either accepts). Hard-coded trunk on one side: trunk forms if the other side accepts it (auto or desirable).
aspect_key: desirable plus any mode forms trunk; auto plus auto does not because neither initiates
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: How does an unmanaged switch differ from a managed switch and why does VLAN configuration require a managed switch?
choice_a: Unmanaged switches are Layer 3 devices; managed switches are Layer 2 only; VLANs require Layer 3 processing
choice_b: Unmanaged switches only support ISL trunking which is incompatible with modern 802.1Q VLANs
choice_c: Unmanaged switches are plug-and-play devices with no remote configurability; VLANs require port assignment commands that can only be issued on a managed switch with a CLI or web interface
choice_d: Unmanaged switches use static MAC tables that cannot update dynamically; VLAN configuration requires dynamic MAC learning
explanation: An unmanaged switch has no configuration interface — it just forwards frames out of the box. Creating VLANs and assigning ports requires issuing configuration commands. This requires a managed switch that has a management interface (CLI via SSH/Telnet; or web GUI) and supports VLAN features.
aspect_key: vlan configuration requires managed switch with configurable cli or web interface
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the operational difference between Cisco's term trunking and other vendors' term tagging?
choice_a: Trunking refers to LACP link aggregation on Cisco equipment; tagging refers to 802.1Q VLAN marking on non-Cisco equipment
choice_b: Trunking means carrying multiple VLANs over one link; tagging describes the 802.1Q marking mechanism — different terms for the same concept depending on vendor
choice_c: Trunking is a Layer 2 concept while tagging is Layer 3; they operate at different OSI layers
choice_d: Cisco uses trunking for ISL and tagging for 802.1Q; the two terms describe different encapsulation protocols
explanation: Cisco commonly calls the feature trunk and the port a trunk port. Other vendors (including IEEE documentation) describe the mechanism as tagging — adding a VLAN tag to frames. Both terms describe the same behavior: one physical link carrying traffic for multiple VLANs using frame marking.
aspect_key: trunking (cisco) and tagging (other vendors) describe the same multi-vlan link feature
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md

question: Why would a network administrator assign the same VLAN to ports on two different physical switches?
choice_a: So both switches share the same MAC address table and can forward frames without flooding
choice_b: So a device on SW1 and a device on SW2 can communicate at Layer 2 as if they were on the same switch; requiring a trunk between the switches to carry VLAN-tagged frames between them
choice_c: So the router can route between the VLAN on SW1 and the VLAN on SW2 using a separate physical link for each switch
choice_d: VLANs cannot span multiple switches; each switch must have its own unique VLAN ID
explanation: VLANs can span multiple switches. For VLAN 10 to exist on both SW1 and SW2: the inter-switch link must be a trunk; both switches must have VLAN 10 configured; and devices on VLAN 10 ports of either switch can communicate as one Layer 2 segment.
aspect_key: same vlan on two switches requires trunk between them to carry vlan-tagged frames
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What did early Ethernet networks use before switches and why did this create performance and security problems?
choice_a: Early Ethernet used bridges with four ports; performance was limited by bridge processing speed; security by bridge encryption overhead
choice_b: Early Ethernet used routers in switching mode; performance suffered because IP routing is slower than MAC forwarding
choice_c: Early Ethernet used hubs — shared media where all devices competed for bandwidth; collisions degraded performance as networks grew and all devices could see all traffic creating a security risk
choice_d: Early Ethernet used token-ring topology where one device transmitted at a time; performance suffered from the token wait time
explanation: Early Ethernet: hub-based shared media. All devices in one collision domain — collisions multiplied with device count. Every device received every frame — passive capture trivial. Switches solved both problems: separate collision domains; and targeted frame delivery.
aspect_key: early ethernet used hubs creating shared collision domain and traffic visibility by all
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: A phone is plugged into a switch access port that is also connected to a PC. The port is configured with both an access VLAN for data and a voice VLAN. How does the switch differentiate the traffic?
choice_a: The switch inspects TCP port numbers to separate voice (UDP/RTP) traffic from data (TCP) traffic at Layer 4
choice_b: The switch uses the IP DSCP marking to assign traffic to the data or voice VLAN
choice_c: The IP phone tags its own voice traffic with the voice VLAN ID while the PC sends untagged data traffic; the switch separates them by presence or absence of the 802.1Q tag and its VLAN ID
choice_d: The switch differentiates traffic by source MAC address since IP phones have a different OUI than PCs
explanation: Access port with voice VLAN: the IP phone understands 802.1Q and tags its voice frames with the voice VLAN ID. The PC sends untagged traffic. The switch receives both: tagged frames go to the voice VLAN; untagged frames stay in the access (data) VLAN. This allows one physical port to support both simultaneously.
aspect_key: ip phone tags voice traffic; pc sends untagged; switch separates by tag presence
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md

question: What is the Layer 3 limitation of a standard Layer 2 switch and how does this affect VLAN design?
choice_a: Layer 2 switches cannot process ARP requests so hosts in the same VLAN cannot resolve MAC addresses
choice_b: Layer 2 switches forward based on MAC addresses and have no visibility into IP addresses; this means they cannot route between VLANs requiring a separate Layer 3 device for inter-VLAN traffic
choice_c: Layer 2 switches require an IP address per port making large VLAN deployments cost-prohibitive
choice_d: Layer 2 switches can only support a maximum of 16 VLANs due to their 4-bit VLAN ID field
explanation: Standard Layer 2 switching is MAC-based and VLAN-aware but IP-blind. For traffic to cross from VLAN 10 to VLAN 20 an IP routing decision must be made by a router or Layer 3 switch. This is why VLAN design always plans for a routing device at the VLAN boundary.
aspect_key: layer 2 switch uses mac not ip so inter-vlan traffic requires a layer 3 device
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Explain the DTP hardcoded trunk nuance where a manually configured trunk port may still send DTP messages.
choice_a: A hardcoded trunk port uses DTP desirable mode internally because Cisco IOS treats manual configuration as an active request
choice_b: A hardcoded trunk port sends DTP messages only if the other side is also in manual trunk mode
choice_c: Manually configuring a port as trunk with switchport mode trunk may still allow the port to send DTP frames to the connected Cisco device; the full disabling of DTP requires also issuing switchport nonegotiate
choice_d: Hardcoded trunk ports send DTP messages only to alert the administrator that the port cannot auto-negotiate
explanation: switchport mode trunk hard-codes the port in trunk mode but on Cisco IOS the port may still generate DTP frames. To completely stop DTP messages the additional command switchport nonegotiate is required. Without this; a connected Cisco device could still try to negotiate trunk parameters.
aspect_key: switchport mode trunk still sends dtp frames; nonegotiate required to fully stop dtp
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Why does within-VLAN unknown unicast flooding stay within the VLAN and not escape to other VLANs?
choice_a: Flooding is suppressed on trunk ports to reduce overhead on inter-switch links
choice_b: The MAC table prevents flooding by always having entries for all ports in any VLAN
choice_c: VLANs are separate Layer 2 broadcast domains; the switch only floods out ports that are members of the same VLAN as the ingress port
