choice_d: Flooding is blocked at VLAN boundaries by the router so it cannot propagate to other VLANs
explanation: Flooding respects VLAN membership. When an unknown unicast frame arrives on a VLAN 10 access port the switch floods it out all other VLAN 10 ports (access and trunk ports that carry VLAN 10) — not ports in other VLANs. VLAN isolation is maintained even during flooding.
aspect_key: flooding only reaches ports in the same vlan respecting layer 2 vlan isolation
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the relationship between SSH and Telnet in terms of capability and what was the reason for Telnet's original design?
choice_a: SSH and Telnet provide different functions: Telnet is for file transfer and SSH is for terminal access
choice_b: SSH is a subset of Telnet that adds encryption; the full Telnet protocol includes SSH as an optional security mode
choice_c: Both provide remote CLI access; Telnet was designed in 1969 before modern network security was a priority so encryption was not included; SSH was created later specifically to provide the same functionality with encryption
choice_d: Telnet was deprecated in 1990 and replaced immediately by SSH; no modern devices support Telnet
explanation: Telnet dates back to 1969 when the internet was small and trusted. Encryption was not considered necessary then. SSH was developed later (1995) to provide the same remote terminal capability — encrypted. Telnet still works but its plaintext design makes it unsuitable for modern network management.
aspect_key: telnet designed in 1969 before security concerns; ssh added encryption to same capability
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 10 - Telnet and SSH/Transcript.md

question: What is the effect on the MAC address table when a new device plugs into a port and transmits its first frame?
choice_a: The MAC table entry for the new device's MAC address is deleted to force flooding until the device is verified
choice_b: No change occurs because the MAC table is only updated by ARP replies from routers
choice_c: The switch adds the new device's source MAC address and the port it arrived on as a new entry in the MAC table
choice_d: The switch broadcasts an ARP probe to confirm the new MAC address is unique before adding it to the table
explanation: On the very first frame from a new device: the switch reads the source MAC address and the ingress port number and records them in the MAC table. This learning happens on every incoming frame that has a source MAC not currently in the table.
aspect_key: switch adds source mac and ingress port to mac table on first frame from new device
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the operational security principle behind hard-coding switch port modes?
choice_a: Hard-coding reduces VLAN database replication traffic letting VTP operate more efficiently
choice_b: Hard-coding increases the number of VLANs a switch can support by reserving DTP negotiation overhead
choice_c: Intentional manual port configuration eliminates ambiguity about port roles and reduces the attack surface by preventing unauthorized trunk negotiation
choice_d: Hard-coding is required because DTP is not supported on access ports and switchport mode must always be set explicitly
explanation: When port modes are hard-coded an administrator always knows exactly what every port is doing — access or trunk — and why. DTP negotiation is disabled on access ports. This removes a class of attack (unauthorized trunk formation) and makes the network behavior predictable.
aspect_key: hard-coding port modes eliminates ambiguity and prevents unauthorized trunk negotiation
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Why is it incorrect to say that switching decisions are based on IP addresses for a standard Layer 2 switch?
choice_a: Switching decisions use MAC addresses for Layer 2 forwarding; IP addresses are used by routers at Layer 3; standard switches do not inspect the IP payload
choice_b: Switches do inspect IP addresses but only to verify the VLAN assignment matches the IP subnet before forwarding
choice_c: Switches use IP addresses for unicast forwarding but switch to MAC-based forwarding for broadcast frames
choice_d: Cisco IOS switches always use IP addresses because Cisco IOS is a Layer 3 operating system regardless of switch model
explanation: Layer 2 switches make forwarding decisions based on destination MAC address in the Ethernet header. The IP packet is the payload of the Ethernet frame and the switch does not open it for standard forwarding. Only Layer 3 switches or routers use IP addresses to make routing decisions.
aspect_key: layer 2 switch uses destination mac not ip for forwarding decisions
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: How does deploying VLANs improve network security compared to a flat switched network?
choice_a: VLANs encrypt traffic between departments preventing hosts in other VLANs from decoding captured frames
choice_b: VLANs restrict physical access to switch ports so only authorized personnel can connect devices
choice_c: VLANs segment the network so a host in one VLAN cannot send or receive Layer 2 traffic destined for another VLAN eliminating a large class of Layer 2 attacks and accidental exposure
choice_d: VLANs enable per-VLAN ARP inspection which prevents hosts from receiving traffic not addressed to them
explanation: In a flat network all hosts receive all broadcast traffic and can ARP-spoof or sniff for each other's traffic. VLANs isolate broadcast domains: hosts in different VLANs cannot directly communicate at Layer 2; a router mediates all inter-VLAN traffic providing an inspection point. This reduces the blast radius of a compromised host.
aspect_key: vlans segment layer 2 so hosts in different vlans cannot directly exchange frames
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 16 - VLANs Made Easy/Transcript.md

question: An administrator manages a 3-switch network. Each switch has VLANs 10; 20; and 30 configured. They create VLAN 40 on the VTP server. What happens to the other two VTP client switches?
choice_a: VLAN 40 must be manually created on each client switch because VTP only replicates VLAN deletions
choice_b: VLAN 40 is automatically added to the VLAN database on both VTP client switches and the administrator must still manually assign access ports to VLAN 40 on each switch
choice_c: VLAN 40 is automatically added to both client switches including automatic port assignment based on the VLAN name
choice_d: Nothing happens until the administrator runs copy running-config startup-config on the VTP server to push the update
explanation: VTP propagates the VLAN database (ID and name) to all clients. Creating VLAN 40 on the server causes both clients to add VLAN 40 to their VLAN table automatically. However VTP does not assign ports — the admin must still manually run switchport access vlan 40 on the relevant ports of each switch.
aspect_key: vtp adds vlan 40 to clients automatically but port assignment must still be done manually
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the conceptual difference between the VLAN operating layer and the router's operating layer and why does this matter for traffic between departments?
choice_a: VLANs and routers both operate at Layer 2; the distinction is only marketing terminology
choice_b: VLANs operate at Layer 4 for session tracking; routers operate at Layer 2 for frame delivery
choice_c: VLANs operate at Layer 2 (MAC-based); routers operate at Layer 3 (IP-based); inter-VLAN traffic must be carried from the Layer 2 domain to a router which makes an IP forwarding decision to return it to the correct VLAN
choice_d: VLANs operate at Layer 3 and can route independently; routers are only needed for internet access
explanation: VLANs are a Layer 2 construct: they segment MAC-based broadcast domains. Routers are Layer 3: they forward based on IP prefixes. When Host A (VLAN 10; IP 192.168.10.5) needs to reach Host B (VLAN 20; IP 192.168.20.5) the packet leaves Layer 2 domain VLAN 10; is routed by the router's IP forwarding; and enters Layer 2 domain VLAN 20.
aspect_key: vlans are layer 2; inter-vlan requires router to make layer 3 ip forwarding decision
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the purpose of the 802.1Q tag requirement for VLAN-aware devices on a trunk?
choice_a: Any device that does not understand 802.1Q will see the VLAN tag as data corruption and drop the frame; VLAN-aware devices strip the tag for processing
choice_b: 802.1Q tags are only required on router interfaces; switches can forward tagged frames without reading the tag
choice_c: VLAN-aware devices must add a second tag for ISL encapsulation before forwarding 802.1Q frames
choice_d: 802.1Q tags force all devices on the trunk to operate at the same speed
explanation: The 802.1Q tag is non-standard to older devices. A device that does not know about VLAN tagging may misinterpret the tag bytes as part of the frame payload or reject the frame as malformed. Trunk links must connect VLAN-aware devices that understand how to insert; read; and strip 802.1Q tags correctly.
aspect_key: non-vlan-aware devices cannot process 802.1q tags and will misinterpret or drop tagged frames
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md

question: How does the subnet connectivity role of a router differ from the VLAN-segmentation role of a switch?
choice_a: Routers create VLANs; switches route between them
choice_b: Routers assign MAC addresses to each subnet; switches assign IP addresses to each VLAN
choice_c: A switch creates logical Layer 2 separation between VLANs; a router connects those separate IP subnets enabling traffic to flow between them at Layer 3
choice_d: Routers and switches perform identical functions — the distinction is only the number of ports
explanation: Switch role: create VLANs as separate Layer 2 broadcast domains. Router role: connect those VLANs' IP subnets together at Layer 3. Each device has a complementary role in a properly segmented network — switches segment; routers connect.
aspect_key: switch segments vlans at layer 2; router connects their ip subnets at layer 3
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: When is Ethernet considered to have overcome legacy networking systems like ARCNET and Token Ring?
choice_a: In the 1960s when the ARPANET adopted Ethernet as its standard link-layer protocol
choice_b: Token Ring and ARCNET are still in use today in industrial networks and have not been fully replaced
choice_c: Ethernet was invented in the late 1970s; became commercially available in 1980; and eventually displaced competing systems like ARCNET and Token Ring due to its scalability and cost advantages
choice_d: Token Ring defeated Ethernet in enterprise networks until 802.11 Wi-Fi made both obsolete simultaneously
explanation: Ethernet was invented at Xerox PARC in the late 1970s and commercialized in 1980. Over the following years it outcompeted alternatives like ARCNET and Token Ring because it scaled better and became cheaper. It is now the dominant wired LAN standard.
aspect_key: ethernet invented 1970s commercialized 1980 eventually replaced arcnet and token ring
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: On a shared Ethernet segment a host receives a frame. Its destination MAC does not match the host's own MAC. What does the host do in normal operation vs. promiscuous mode?
choice_a: Normal operation: the host processes the frame and replies; promiscuous mode: the host drops the frame silently
choice_b: Normal operation and promiscuous mode behave identically for non-matching frames
choice_c: Normal operation: the host discards the frame; promiscuous mode: the host passes the frame to the OS for capture and analysis
choice_d: Normal operation: the host sends an ICMP reject; promiscuous mode: the host forwards the frame to the next device
explanation: Ethernet packet screening: in normal operation a host checks the destination MAC; if it does not match the host's own MAC or the broadcast address the frame is discarded at the NIC level. In promiscuous mode (used by Wireshark and similar tools) the NIC passes all frames to the OS regardless of destination MAC — enabling passive traffic capture.
aspect_key: normal mode discards non-matching frames; promiscuous mode captures all frames
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the VPN tunnel concept and why is it called a tunnel?
choice_a: VPN tunnels are called tunnels because they traverse underground fiber optic cable infrastructure
choice_b: VPN tunnel refers to the logical path through a router's internal routing table from source to destination subnet
choice_c: A VPN is called a tunnel because the encrypted connection creates an isolated protected path through the public internet; from an outsider's view traffic appears as opaque encrypted data traversing the public network
choice_d: VPN tunnels are called tunnels because they encapsulate IPv4 packets inside IPv6 for compatibility across dual-stack networks
explanation: A VPN tunnel encapsulates encrypted data inside packets that cross the public internet. The contents are opaque to anyone along the path — only the endpoints can decrypt them. The analogy to a physical tunnel: the path passes through shared space but the contents are isolated and protected from the surrounding environment.
aspect_key: vpn tunnel creates encrypted isolated path through the public internet
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: A switch port connects to a second switch rather than a single host. What are the implications for the MAC address table and forwarding?
choice_a: The switch can only store one MAC per port so traffic from the downstream switch will be dropped after the first host is learned
choice_b: The switch stores multiple MAC addresses on the port that leads to the downstream switch one for each host reachable through it
choice_c: The switch disables learning on uplink ports and always floods inter-switch frames
choice_d: The switch creates a separate MAC table for each switch in the network to avoid MAC address conflicts
explanation: A switch port leading to another switch will have multiple MAC addresses associated with it — one for each host that is reachable through that link. The switch records all of them as learned on the upstream port. This is how MAC tables scale to multi-switch networks.
aspect_key: switch learns multiple mac addresses on an uplink port one per reachable downstream host
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the administrative advantage of manually creating VLANs and assigning ports rather than relying on automatic or dynamic VLAN assignment?
choice_a: Automatic VLAN assignment is more accurate than manual assignment because it uses the device MAC database
choice_b: Manual creation gives administrators explicit control over which devices belong to which broadcast domain preventing accidental or unauthorized VLAN membership
choice_c: Manual VLAN assignment is required because dynamic assignment is not supported on any managed switch
choice_d: Automatic VLAN assignment requires a VTP server which is a single point of failure; manual assignment avoids this dependency
explanation: VLAN membership determines what a device can and cannot reach at Layer 2. Explicit manual assignment ensures the administrator intentionally controls this access. Misassignment means a device might land in the wrong broadcast domain accessing resources it should not. Manual control is part of the security discipline of VLAN design.
aspect_key: manual vlan and port assignment gives explicit control over broadcast domain membership
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: How does a switch bridge two different link speeds such as a 1 Gbps host port and a 100 Mbps printer port?
choice_a: The switch reduces the host port speed to 100 Mbps to match the printer and prevents loss
choice_b: The switch requires both ports to run at the same speed and will not forward frames if speeds differ
choice_c: The switch buffers incoming frames in memory adapting to the speed difference so both devices can operate at their native rates and frames are not dropped due to the speed gap
choice_d: The switch sends the frame at 1 Gbps to the 100 Mbps printer and the printer buffers the excess
explanation: Switch buffers allow speed adaptation. A 1 Gbps frame arriving for a 100 Mbps destination port is stored in the switch's buffer. The switch then transmits it out the 100 Mbps port at that port's native rate. This is one of the fundamental functions of switch buffering — absorbing speed mismatches between ports.
aspect_key: switch buffers frames to adapt between different link speeds on ingress and egress ports
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What happens to a Telnet session when an attacker runs a packet capture tool on the same network segment?
choice_a: The attacker can capture the TCP segments but cannot read the content because Telnet compresses its payload
choice_b: The attacker captures only the packet headers; the Telnet session body is encrypted by the OS TCP stack
choice_c: The attacker must send a MITM redirect first; a passive capture cannot read Telnet because the session uses challenge-response authentication
choice_d: The attacker captures the packets and can read all usernames; passwords; commands; and output in plaintext from the captured data
explanation: Telnet sends everything in cleartext. A network capture tool (Wireshark; tcpdump) on the same segment captures all Telnet packets and the payload — which is the entire terminal session — is human-readable. No decryption needed. This is the fundamental reason Telnet is inappropriate for managing network infrastructure.
aspect_key: telnet session is fully readable in packet captures including all credentials and commands
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 10 - Telnet and SSH/Transcript.md

question: What is the conceptual relationship between the switch VLAN database and the trunk link in enabling the same VLAN to span two switches?
choice_a: The VLAN database on each switch independently assigns IP addresses that the trunk link uses to route between VLANs
choice_b: The trunk link carries all VLAN traffic as untagged frames and the receiving switch reassigns the VLAN based on its local port mapping
choice_c: Both switches must have the VLAN ID in their VLAN database; the 802.1Q trunk link carries tagged frames preserving the VLAN ID so the receiving switch delivers frames to the correct VLAN ports
choice_d: The VLAN database is only needed on the VTP server; the trunk link propagates VLAN membership to client switch ports automatically
explanation: For VLAN 10 to span two switches: (1) VLAN 10 must exist in both switches' VLAN databases (via VTP or manual config); (2) the inter-switch link must be a trunk; (3) the trunk must allow VLAN 10; (4) the trunk uses 802.1Q tags to identify which frames belong to VLAN 10. Without all three elements the VLAN cannot span both switches.
aspect_key: vlan must exist in both databases and trunk must carry tagged frames for vlan to span switches
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md

question: A switch has three ports in VLAN 10 (ports 1-3) and two ports in VLAN 20 (ports 4-5). A broadcast arrives on port 1. Which ports receive the frame and why do ports 4 and 5 not receive it?
choice_a: Ports 2; 3; 4; and 5 receive the broadcast because broadcasts always reach all switch ports
choice_b: Only port 1 receives a copy because the switch mirrors broadcasts to the source port
choice_c: Ports 2 and 3 receive the broadcast because VLANs are separate Layer 2 broadcast domains and the switch only floods within VLAN 10
choice_d: Ports 2; 3; and 4 receive the broadcast; port 5 is excluded as it is furthest from port 1
explanation: Broadcast flooding respects VLAN membership. The frame arrives in VLAN 10 (port 1). The switch floods it out all other VLAN 10 ports (ports 2 and 3). Ports 4 and 5 are in VLAN 20 — a separate broadcast domain. They do not participate in VLAN 10 broadcasts. VLAN segmentation is the mechanism that stops cross-VLAN flooding.
aspect_key: vlan 10 broadcast floods only to vlan 10 ports; vlan 20 ports are a separate domain
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 16 - VLANs Made Easy/Transcript.md

question: SW1 has one trunk link to SW2. Both switches have VLANs 10; 20; and 30. A packet enters SW1 on a VLAN 10 access port and needs to reach a host in VLAN 20 on SW2. What path does it take and what devices participate?
choice_a: The packet crosses the SW1-SW2 trunk tagged as VLAN 10; SW2 strips the tag and forwards it to the VLAN 20 port
choice_b: The packet crosses the trunk; SW2 performs inter-VLAN routing and delivers it to VLAN 20 without a router
choice_c: The packet stays in VLAN 10 and the destination host's VLAN 20 assignment is temporarily changed to allow the delivery
choice_d: The packet leaves SW1 to a router (via VLAN 10); the router routes it to VLAN 20; the router sends it back to SW2 via a VLAN 20 link; SW2 delivers it to the destination host on a VLAN 20 access port
explanation: Layer 2 cannot cross VLAN boundaries. The packet: (1) exits SW1 on VLAN 10 toward the default gateway router; (2) router strips L2; inspects IP destination; routes to VLAN 20 subnet; (3) router sends frame tagged or over VLAN 20 link to SW2; (4) SW2 delivers to the VLAN 20 access port. A router is mandatory in the path.
aspect_key: inter-vlan cross-switch traffic requires a router; switches alone cannot route between vlans
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: An administrator notices that after a network move a switch port connecting to a downstream switch has hundreds of MAC addresses in the MAC table but the previous port only had a few. What does this indicate and is it a problem?
choice_a: Hundreds of MAC addresses on one port indicates a broadcast storm and must be remediated immediately by disabling the port
choice_b: Multiple MAC addresses on one uplink port is normal — each reflects one host reachable through the downstream switch; the count growing with more downstream hosts is expected behavior
choice_c: Multiple MAC addresses on one port is a sign of a MAC address table overflow attack and indicates all frames are being flooded
choice_d: Multiple MAC addresses on one port indicates a misconfigured trunk which is sending all VLAN traffic unsegregated
explanation: A switch port connected to another switch learns a MAC address for every host that communicates through that link. Hundreds of MACs on an uplink port in a multi-switch network is normal and expected. It would only be a concern if unknown addresses appear (rogue devices) or if the numbers exceed expected host counts.
aspect_key: multiple macs on uplink port is normal; each represents one reachable downstream host
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: A router-on-a-stick configuration has subinterface fa0/0.10 with encapsulation dot1Q 10 and IP 192.168.10.1 and subinterface fa0/0.20 with encapsulation dot1Q 20 and IP 192.168.20.1. A host in VLAN 10 (IP 192.168.10.5) sends a packet to a host in VLAN 20 (IP 192.168.20.10). Trace the exact Layer 2 and Layer 3 steps.
choice_a: The switch inspects the IP destination and routes between VLANs directly without involving the router
choice_b: Host sends untagged frame; router receives it; adds VLAN 20 tag; sends back to the switch; switch delivers to VLAN 20 host
choice_c: Host ARPs for gateway 192.168.10.1; gets router MAC; sends Ethernet frame (src: host10 MAC; dst: router MAC; VLAN 10 tag on trunk) to router; router decapsulates; routes to 192.168.20.10; ARP for host20 MAC; sends Ethernet frame (VLAN 20 tag) to switch; switch delivers to host20 access port stripped of tag
choice_d: Host sends frame directly to VLAN 20 destination IP; switch sees the IP and forwards it using VLAN 20 MAC table entry
explanation: Step-by-step: (1) host10 ARPs for 192.168.10.1 (gateway); switch forwards to trunk; router responds on fa0/0.10; (2) host10 sends IP packet in Ethernet frame (dst MAC = router; dot1Q VLAN 10 tag on trunk); (3) router receives on fa0/0.10; strips L2; sees IP dst 192.168.20.10; routes to fa0/0.20; (4) router ARPs for host20 on VLAN 20; (5) router sends frame tagged VLAN 20; switch strips tag on access port; delivers to host20.
aspect_key: router-on-a-stick inter-vlan routing path involves arp gateway mac routing and re-tagging
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md

question: Why does a MAC address table overflow attack (MAC flooding) cause a switch to behave like a hub and what is the security consequence?
choice_a: MAC flooding removes all VLAN assignments from the MAC table causing the switch to assign all ports to VLAN 1 and flood all frames within VLAN 1
choice_b: MAC flooding causes the switch CPU to restart which temporarily disables all 802.1Q tagging turning trunk ports into access ports
choice_c: When the MAC table is full the switch cannot learn new MAC addresses and must flood all subsequent unknown unicast frames out all ports in the VLAN allowing an attacker on any port to capture traffic intended for other devices
choice_d: MAC flooding corrupts the FCS on all frames causing all devices to retransmit simultaneously overwhelming the switch buffer
explanation: Switch MAC tables have finite size. An attacker sends thousands of frames with random source MACs filling the table. Now when legitimate frames arrive the switch cannot find the destination in the (full) table so it floods — like a hub. All devices in the VLAN receive all unicast traffic enabling passive capture. Port-security mitigates this by limiting learned MACs per port.
aspect_key: mac table overflow forces flooding of all unicast frames enabling traffic capture
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: How does NAT return traffic work in detail when multiple internal hosts simultaneously access the same external web server?
choice_a: The router sends all replies to the host that made the most recent request dropping the others
choice_b: The router uses the VLAN ID from the original request to identify which internal host should receive each reply
choice_c: The router broadcasts each reply to all internal hosts and each host accepts only the packet with its own IP
choice_d: The router tracks source IP; source port; and translated port for each session so each reply is uniquely mapped back to the correct internal host using the port-session table
explanation: NAT maintains a session table: each entry stores (internal IP; internal port; external IP; external port; translated port). Multiple hosts contacting the same server will each have a unique translated port. When replies arrive the router matches the destination port to the session table and restores the correct internal IP and port before forwarding inward.
aspect_key: nat uses port-session table to demultiplex replies from same server to multiple internal hosts
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: A network has departments A and B on the same physical switch. Department A must never be able to send or receive traffic from Department B at any layer. What is the design requirement and why is VLAN isolation alone insufficient?
choice_a: VLANs alone are sufficient because they prevent all Layer 2 and Layer 3 communication between the segments
choice_b: NAT between VLANs provides absolute isolation; VLAN segmentation is only needed for broadcast reduction
choice_c: Assign both departments to different VLANs with different subnets and do not configure inter-VLAN routing between them; VLAN isolation prevents Layer 2 communication; and without a routing path there is no Layer 3 path either — combined this achieves network-layer isolation; physical isolation is only needed for absolute air-gap requirements
choice_d: VLAN isolation is sufficient at Layer 2 but each VLAN needs its own dedicated switch to prevent Layer 1 crosstalk
explanation: VLANs isolate Layer 2. Without inter-VLAN routing (no router between the VLANs; no Layer 3 switch routing between them) there is also no Layer 3 path. Together this achieves network isolation. If absolute air-gap is needed (no shared hardware at all) then physically separate switches are required — but that is not usually mandated by network policy alone.
aspect_key: vlan plus no routing path achieves network isolation; air-gap requires physical separation
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 16 - VLANs Made Easy/Transcript.md

question: What is the full sequence of DTP negotiation outcomes for all three mode combinations and what is the hardcoded trunk caveat?
choice_a: Auto+Auto forms a trunk; Desirable+Auto does not; Desirable+Desirable forms a trunk; hardcoded trunks never send DTP
choice_b: Auto+Auto forms a trunk; Desirable+Auto forms a trunk; Desirable+Desirable forms a trunk; hardcoded trunks suppress all DTP messages
choice_c: Auto+Auto does not form a trunk; Desirable+Auto forms a trunk; Desirable+Desirable forms a trunk; hardcoded trunks may still send DTP frames unless switchport nonegotiate is added
choice_d: Auto+Auto forms a trunk only if VLANs match; Desirable+Auto always forms a trunk; Desirable+Desirable requires VTP server acknowledgment
explanation: DTP outcomes: Auto+Auto = no trunk (neither side negotiates). Desirable+Auto = trunk (desirable initiates; auto accepts). Desirable+Desirable = trunk (both negotiate). Hardcoded (switchport mode trunk): port is a trunk but Cisco IOS may still send DTP frames. switchport nonegotiate suppresses all DTP messages from hardcoded trunk ports.
aspect_key: auto-auto no trunk; desirable-auto trunk; desirable-desirable trunk; hardcoded still sends dtp
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: How does VTP's revision number system become a catastrophic risk when a used switch is added to a production network?
choice_a: VTP revision numbers cause the switch to block all trunk links until a new election cycle completes disrupting production traffic
choice_b: VTP revision numbers are only used for authentication; a used switch with a known revision number cannot affect the VLAN database
choice_c: A used switch with a higher VTP revision number will push its VLAN database to all VTP client switches in the domain overwriting the production VLAN database and potentially deleting all existing VLANs causing widespread outages
choice_d: VTP revision numbers only affect newly created VLANs; existing production VLANs are protected from overwrite by the revision system
explanation: VTP uses revision numbers to determine which database is most recent. VTP clients accept the highest revision number they see. If a used switch with many prior VLAN configurations has a higher revision number it becomes the authoritative source — all VTP clients adopt its VLAN database. If that database lacks production VLANs they are deleted causing switch ports to lose VLAN assignments. Mitigation: reset revision number to 0 by changing VTP domain before connecting to production.
aspect_key: used switch with higher revision number can overwrite production vlan database via vtp
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Explain why SSH public key authentication is more secure than password authentication for managing network infrastructure.
choice_a: Public key authentication uses longer key lengths than passwords making brute force slower but both are equally vulnerable to packet capture
choice_b: Public key authentication is more convenient but no more secure; the private key is transmitted to the server during authentication
choice_c: Public key authentication eliminates the transmission of a secret over the network entirely — the server verifies a mathematical challenge using the stored public key; the private key never leaves the client device; even a fully captured session reveals nothing that can be used to authenticate
choice_d: Public key authentication is less secure because anyone with the public key can impersonate the administrator
explanation: Password auth: the password is sent to the server (encrypted in SSH but still transmitted). A compromised server could expose it. Public key auth: the server stores only the public key. Authentication works by the client proving possession of the private key via a mathematical challenge-response. The private key never leaves the client. Even if the server is compromised the public key cannot be used to authenticate anywhere else.
aspect_key: public key auth proves possession without transmitting the secret; password is transmitted
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 10 - Telnet and SSH/Transcript.md

question: An IP phone and a PC both connect through one switch port. The switch is configured with access VLAN 10 for data and voice VLAN 50 for voice. The IP phone is not tagging traffic. What will happen and how is this diagnosed?
choice_a: The phone's untagged traffic will be placed in VLAN 10 with the PC traffic; voice quality will degrade due to shared bandwidth
choice_b: The phone and PC traffic will be blocked because the switch requires tagged traffic on all ports with a voice VLAN configured
choice_c: The phone's untagged traffic will be placed in VLAN 50 automatically because the voice VLAN is always the higher priority
choice_d: The switch will err-disable the port because untagged traffic from the phone conflicts with the PC data VLAN
explanation: If the IP phone does not send tagged voice traffic (misconfiguration; wrong firmware; or CDP not working to inform the phone) all of its traffic will be untagged and fall into the data VLAN 10 along with the PC. Voice and data will share the same VLAN and QoS queue. Diagnosis: check show interfaces <id> for voice VLAN and verify the phone's VLAN assignment with show cdp neighbors.
aspect_key: ip phone sending untagged traffic falls into data vlan degrading voice quality
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md

question: A frame arrives on a trunk port tagged with VLAN 40 but VLAN 40 is not in the switch's VLAN database. What does the switch do and what administrative action caused this?
choice_a: The switch forwards the frame to all ports and lets hosts determine if VLAN 40 applies to them
choice_b: The switch creates VLAN 40 dynamically so the frame is delivered correctly
choice_c: The switch drops the frame; VLAN 40 traffic was likely allowed on the trunk because it was not pruned; the administrator forgot to create VLAN 40 on this switch after creating it on the VTP server
choice_d: The switch sends the frame to the router on the native VLAN for VLAN 40 resolution
explanation: If a VLAN exists on the trunk's allowed list but is not created in the switch's VLAN database the switch drops frames for that VLAN — it has no VLAN membership to associate the traffic with. This can happen when VTP propagation fails or when the admin manually creates a VLAN on a server but VTP sync did not complete. Fix: create VLAN 40 on this switch.
aspect_key: frames for uncreated vlans are dropped even if the vlan id is allowed on the trunk
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Compare the Layer 2 impact of replacing a hub with a switch on a 24-device segment in terms of collision domains; concurrent sessions; and device privacy.
choice_a: Both hub and switch have 24 collision domains; the switch improves privacy by encrypting inter-device traffic
choice_b: Hub: 1 collision domain; at most 1 concurrent session (CSMA/CD); all devices see all traffic. Switch: 24 collision domains; up to 12 concurrent full-duplex sessions; each frame goes only to the destination port improving privacy
choice_c: Hub: 1 collision domain; all devices visible to each other. Switch: 1 collision domain; frames still reach all devices but privacy is improved by limiting flooding
choice_d: Hub and switch have identical collision domains; the switch improvement is speed only
explanation: Hub: all 24 devices in 1 collision domain; CSMA/CD allows only 1 transmission at a time across the entire segment; all devices receive all traffic (privacy risk). Switch: 24 separate collision domains (1 per port link); multiple simultaneous point-to-point sessions; frames sent only to destination port protecting privacy and multiplying available bandwidth.
aspect_key: switch creates 24 collision domains enables concurrent sessions and limits frame visibility
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: An attacker plugs into a switch port in DTP desirable mode and sends DTP negotiate frames. What sequence of events leads to VLAN hopping access and what is the most effective mitigation?
choice_a: The attacker gains access to VLAN 1 only because trunk negotiation always places the attacker in the native VLAN regardless of tag crafting
choice_b: DTP desirable mode cannot form a trunk without administrator confirmation so the attack fails at the negotiation phase
choice_c: Attacker sends DTP frames; port in desirable mode accepts and transitions to trunk mode; attacker configures 802.1Q on their device; attacker sends and receives tagged frames for all VLANs on the trunk; mitigation: configure switchport mode access on all end-user ports
choice_d: The attacker can form a trunk only if the VTP domain name matches; changing the VTP domain name mitigates trunk negotiation attacks
explanation: Attack sequence: (1) attacker sends DTP negotiation frames from rogue device; (2) switch port in desirable mode responds and both sides agree to trunk; (3) attacker configures 802.1Q on their NIC or software; (4) attacker receives all VLAN traffic on the trunk. Mitigation: switchport mode access disables DTP entirely on that port — no negotiation is possible.
aspect_key: dtp desirable port negotiates trunk with attacker; fixed by hard-coding access mode
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: How does ARP interact with the switch MAC table in a scenario where Host A (192.168.1.10) sends the first packet ever to Host B (192.168.1.20) both in VLAN 10?
choice_a: Host A sends IP packet directly; the switch routes it using the destination IP ignoring MAC
choice_b: Host A sends a unicast ARP request to Host B's IP; the switch forwards it directly using MAC; Host B responds
choice_c: Host A broadcasts an ARP request with destination MAC FF-FF-FF-FF-FF-FF asking who has 192.168.1.20; the switch floods it within VLAN 10; Host B replies with its MAC; the switch learns Host B's MAC on its port; Host A now has Host B's MAC and sends the unicast IP packet directly
choice_d: Host A sends the IP packet to the router default gateway which ARP-resolves Host B's MAC and delivers it
explanation: ARP-to-switch interaction: (1) Host A has no MAC for 192.168.1.20; broadcasts ARP (dst FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) in VLAN 10; (2) switch sees ARP on Host A's port — learns Host A MAC; floods ARP out all VLAN 10 ports; (3) Host B receives ARP; replies unicast to Host A MAC — switch learns Host B MAC on its port; (4) Host A now has Host B's MAC; sends unicast frame; switch forwards directly.
aspect_key: arp broadcast triggers flooding then learning so future unicast frames are forwarded directly
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: A company grows from one switch to five switches with a shared inter-switch trunk infrastructure. All switches are in one large flat VLAN 1. What performance and security problems emerge and what is the solution?
choice_a: The primary problem is switch CPU overload from managing five separate MAC tables; VLANs fix this by merging MAC tables
choice_b: Flat VLAN 1 across five switches means one large broadcast domain; all ARP; DHCP; and broadcast traffic from all hosts reaches all devices simultaneously consuming bandwidth; any host can sniff or spoof any other at Layer 2; solution is to segment into multiple VLANs with routers providing inter-VLAN routing
choice_c: Flat VLAN 1 causes spanning-tree instability because all five switches compete for root bridge; VLANs fix this by running per-switch spanning tree
choice_d: Flat VLAN 1 forces all five switches into DTP auto mode creating uncontrolled trunk negotiation across the domain
explanation: Five switches in one flat VLAN 1: broadcasts (ARP; DHCP; etc.) flood all five switches reaching every host regardless of department. A sniffer anywhere captures all broadcast traffic and potentially unicast too (via ARP poisoning). Performance: as hosts grow broadcasts consume a growing share of bandwidth. Solution: segment into multiple VLANs matching department boundaries with routers/Layer 3 switches handling inter-VLAN traffic.
aspect_key: flat vlan 1 across all switches creates large broadcast domain with security and performance problems
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 16 - VLANs Made Easy/Transcript.md

question: What are the exact Layer 2 events when Host A on SW1 sends traffic to Host B on SW2 both in VLAN 10 connected by a trunk link and Host B's MAC is unknown to SW1?
choice_a: Host A sends a tagged frame; SW1 strips the tag; SW2 looks up the destination MAC and forwards to Host B
choice_b: Host A sends a unicast frame; SW1 checks its MAC table; finds Host B unknown; floods within VLAN 10 including out the trunk (802.1Q-tagged); SW2 receives the tagged frame; recognizes it as VLAN 10; floods it to all VLAN 10 ports; Host B replies; SW1 and SW2 both learn Host B's MAC
choice_c: SW1 queries SW2's MAC table over the trunk before forwarding to avoid flooding
choice_d: Host A sends a broadcast which SW1 forwards untagged to SW2; SW2 converts it to unicast using its own MAC table
explanation: Event sequence: (1) SW1 receives frame from Host A on VLAN 10 access port; learns Host A MAC; (2) destination Host B MAC is unknown to SW1; (3) SW1 floods within VLAN 10 — includes all VLAN 10 access ports and the trunk; (4) frame exits SW1 trunk tagged 802.1Q VLAN 10; (5) SW2 receives tagged frame; VLAN 10 match; floods to all VLAN 10 access ports; (6) Host B receives; replies; (7) both switches learn Host B's MAC on its respective port.
aspect_key: unknown unicast floods across trunk tagged; both switches learn source macs from reply
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md

question: Why does a standard Layer 2 switch not need an IP address to forward Ethernet frames yet still requires one in practice?
choice_a: Switches need an IP address because MAC address lookup is replaced by IP routing in the switch hardware
choice_b: Switches need an IP address because 802.1Q tagging is an IP-layer operation requiring an IP source address in the tag
choice_c: A switch forwards frames based on MAC addresses only and does not need an IP address to do so; however managed switches need a management IP address (configured on an SVI) to allow administrators to reach the switch remotely via Telnet or SSH for configuration and monitoring
choice_d: Switches need an IP address only when VLANs are configured because VLAN frames require an IP header for VLAN ID resolution
explanation: Layer 2 forwarding is MAC-based — no IP needed. But without a management IP no one can SSH or Telnet to the switch to configure it; troubleshoot it; or monitor it remotely. Administrators configure an IP on the management VLAN SVI (interface vlan 1) and set a default gateway so the switch is reachable from anywhere on the network.
aspect_key: switch forwards by mac without ip but needs management ip for remote administration
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: A network administrator realizes that the Cisco switch connecting the accounting VLAN to the rest of the network has its port in DTP auto mode. Accounting contains financial data. Describe the threat and the full remediation.
choice_a: Change the native VLAN on the trunk and add a firewall ACL to block inter-VLAN accounting access
choice_b: The threat is ARP poisoning; remediation is to enable dynamic ARP inspection on the accounting VLAN
choice_c: The threat is an attacker on any port adjacent to accounting negotiating a trunk and receiving all accounting VLAN traffic; remediation: set all access ports to switchport mode access; set all trunk ports to switchport mode trunk and switchport nonegotiate; move native VLAN to an unused VLAN to prevent double-tag hopping
choice_d: The threat is VTP revision number corruption; remediation is to set VTP transparent mode on the accounting switch
explanation: Threat: any device connected to an auto-mode port can trigger DTP trunk formation — including ports adjacent to accounting. Once trunked the attacker receives 802.1Q-tagged frames for all VLANs including accounting. Remediation: (1) switchport mode access on all access ports (disables DTP); (2) switchport mode trunk + switchport nonegotiate on all uplinks (disables DTP response); (3) change native VLAN to unused ID (prevents double-tagging attack).
aspect_key: dtp auto on accounting ports enables trunk negotiation; fix by hard-coding access and nonegotiate
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Explain the full role of Ethernet frame screening on a shared hub segment and why switches fundamentally change the privacy model.
choice_a: On a hub all devices process all frames and software decides which to keep; on a switch all devices still receive all frames but hardware discards non-matching frames before software can see them
choice_b: On a hub any device can enter promiscuous mode and capture all traffic; on a switch frames are only forwarded to the destination port so a device in promiscuous mode on an access port sees only its own unicast traffic and broadcast/multicast — it cannot capture other unicast sessions
choice_c: On a hub frames are encrypted so promiscuous mode reveals only garbled data; on a switch frames are cleartext but promiscuous mode is disabled in hardware
choice_d: Hubs and switches provide identical privacy; the difference is only collision handling not frame distribution
explanation: Hub model: every frame reaches every NIC. NIC in promiscuous mode captures all of it. Any host can sniff any other. Switch model: the switch uses the MAC table to send unicast frames only to the destination port. A host in promiscuous mode on that port sees only: (1) frames addressed to it; (2) broadcasts; (3) flooded unknown unicast. It cannot see unicast sessions between other hosts. Fundamentally different privacy model.
aspect_key: switch sends unicast only to destination port so promiscuous mode cannot capture other sessions
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: How does the VTP limitation that it does not assign access ports combine with the VTP revision risk to create a dangerous operational scenario?
choice_a: These two limitations cancel each other out — VTP revision risk only applies when VTP assigns ports which it never does
choice_b: VTP revision wipes the VLAN database silently; since VTP also assigns ports both the VLAN and port assignment are lost simultaneously; the network never recovers automatically
choice_c: A used switch introduced with a higher revision number wipes the VLAN database on all VTP clients; port assignments (which VTP never managed) survive on each switch but now point to VLANs that no longer exist causing all access ports to become inactive in unknown VLANs
choice_d: VTP revision risk does not apply when VLAN port assignment is manual because the switch detects a conflict
explanation: Combined effect: (1) Used switch introduced with high revision wipes VLAN database — all VLAN IDs and names deleted from all VTP clients; (2) Access port assignments (not managed by VTP; set manually) still reference the deleted VLAN IDs; (3) Ports referencing nonexistent VLANs become inactive — connected devices lose network access; (4) Administrator must recreate all VLANs and re-verify every port assignment across all switches.
aspect_key: vtp revision wipe deletes vlans while manual port assignments reference deleted vlans causing outage
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: An SFP module for 8 Gbps Fibre Channel is inserted into a switch's SFP port. A workstation using a standard copper 1 Gbps NIC is plugged into an access port on the same switch. Describe how the switch bridges these two media types.
choice_a: The switch cannot bridge Fibre Channel and Ethernet because they are different Layer 2 protocols and require a gateway
choice_b: The switch requires both ports to run at the same speed and automatically down-negotiates the SFP to 1 Gbps to match the workstation
choice_c: The switch accepts frames from the copper 1 Gbps port; stores them in buffers; and forwards them using the SFP at 8 Gbps if the destination is reachable through that uplink; MAC-based forwarding decisions are media-independent
choice_d: The switch merges both port speeds and allocates 4.5 Gbps to each link for fairness
explanation: The switch is media-independent at Layer 2. MAC-based forwarding works the same regardless of whether the physical layer is copper; fiber; or an SFP module. Frames arriving on the 1 Gbps copper port are stored in memory; the MAC table lookup identifies the egress port; frames are then transmitted on the 8 Gbps SFP. Buffering absorbs the speed difference.
aspect_key: switch forwards by mac independent of media type; buffers handle speed differences between ports
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Describe a scenario where the same physical department needs devices in two different VLANs and explain the design and operational implications.
choice_a: Two VLANs on same ports is impossible; VLANs are physical and each port can only carry one department's traffic
choice_b: One switch can only hold one VLAN so the department would need two physical switches
choice_c: A device can only belong to one access VLAN — the administrator must decide which VLAN each device belongs to by port assignment; if a device legitimately needs to communicate in both VLANs it requires a trunk NIC or two physical connections; alternatively the router provides routed access between VLANs
choice_d: Two VLANs in one physical department require duplicate cabling because VLAN tags are stripped before reaching the device
explanation: Access ports carry one VLAN. If a device needs to be in two VLANs it needs either: (1) a trunk NIC (802.1Q-capable server NIC configured with subinterfaces); or (2) two physical connections each to a different VLAN. Ordinary workstations cannot be in two VLANs simultaneously. Typically departments are mapped to one VLAN and inter-VLAN routing provides cross-department access.
aspect_key: access ports carry one vlan; dual-vlan devices need trunk nic or two physical connections
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: A network admin attempts to configure SSH on a new Cisco switch but gets an error that RSA keys cannot be generated. What prerequisites are most likely missing?
choice_a: The switch is missing a management IP and cannot generate RSA keys without a configured IP address
choice_b: RSA key generation requires that VTP be set to server mode and the management VLAN be active
choice_c: RSA key generation requires a hostname other than the default and an ip domain-name to be configured; without these IOS cannot construct the key's distinguished name
choice_d: RSA keys cannot be generated on Layer 2 switches; SSH is a router-only feature in Cisco IOS
explanation: Cisco IOS SSH RSA key generation requirement: the switch must have (1) a non-default hostname (crypto key generate rsa uses hostname as part of the key name) and (2) an ip domain-name configured. Without both IOS refuses to generate RSA keys. Additionally the switch needs an IP on a management SVI and VTY lines configured for transport input ssh.
aspect_key: ssh rsa key generation requires hostname and ip domain-name before crypto key generate rsa
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 10 - Telnet and SSH/Transcript.md

question: What is the exact security difference between VTP transparent mode and VTP client mode and when would an administrator choose transparent mode?
choice_a: VTP transparent mode and client mode are identical; the only difference is that transparent mode requires manual VLAN creation while client mode accepts VTP updates
choice_b: VTP transparent mode receives VTP updates from the server and stores them locally but does not apply them to its VLAN database; client mode applies updates and also forwards them
choice_c: VTP transparent mode does not synchronize with the VTP domain — it maintains its own independent VLAN database and forwards VTP messages without applying them; client mode accepts and applies VTP updates; transparent mode is chosen when the switch should not risk receiving a revision-number wipe
choice_d: VTP transparent mode is only available on Layer 3 switches; Layer 2 switches must use client or server mode
explanation: VTP transparent mode: switch passes VTP advertisements to adjacent switches (so they still reach clients) but does not apply them to its own VLAN database. It maintains a locally configured VLAN database independently. Used when: switch should be isolated from VTP revision-number wipeouts; or when the switch has unique local VLANs not in the domain database.
aspect_key: vtp transparent mode passes updates without applying them protecting local vlan database
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Describe the forwarding decision process a switch makes for a frame arriving on a trunk port with VLAN tag 30 destined for MAC AA-BB-CC-00-00-01 which is in the MAC table on an access port in VLAN 10.
choice_a: The switch strips the tag; forwards the frame to the access port in VLAN 10 because the MAC matches regardless of VLAN
choice_b: The switch performs NAT between VLAN 30 and VLAN 10 allowing the frame to cross VLAN boundaries
choice_c: The switch drops the frame because the destination MAC is in VLAN 10 but the incoming frame is tagged VLAN 30 — MAC table entries are VLAN-scoped and the frame cannot cross VLAN boundaries at Layer 2
choice_d: The switch copies the frame to all ports in VLAN 30 and all ports in VLAN 10 to ensure delivery
explanation: MAC tables are VLAN-scoped. The switch looks up AA-BB-CC-00-00-01 in VLAN 30's table. That MAC is registered in VLAN 10 — not VLAN 30. The lookup finds no match in VLAN 30. The switch either floods within VLAN 30 (unknown unicast) or drops if the destination MAC genuinely does not exist in VLAN 30. It never crosses to VLAN 10 without routing.
aspect_key: mac table is vlan-scoped; frame tagged vlan 30 cannot be forwarded to vlan 10 mac entry
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the fundamental reason that Ethernet replaced ARCNET and Token Ring and what characteristics made it win?
choice_a: Ethernet won because it was the only standard that supported fiber optic cables while ARCNET and Token Ring required coaxial
choice_b: Ethernet won because it used token passing which was more efficient than ARCNET's bus topology
choice_c: Ethernet's combination of relatively simple design; adaptability to higher speeds over decades; cost advantages from commodity hardware production; and eventual support for both copper and fiber gave it commercial and technical advantages that displaced both ARCNET and Token Ring
choice_d: Ethernet won because it was mandated by U.S. government procurement policy which forced adoption across the industry
explanation: Ethernet's architecture scaled from 10 Mbps (1980) through 100 Mbps; 1 Gbps; 10 Gbps; and beyond over decades. Each speed generation used the same framing format enabling backward compatibility. Volume production drove costs down. ARCNET was slower and Token Ring more expensive. Ethernet absorbed these markets by being good enough and cheap enough.
aspect_key: ethernet won through scalability cost and compatibility advantages over arcnet and token ring
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: A routed network has two subnets: 10.0.100.0/24 and 192.168.0.0/24. Host A (10.0.100.5) sends a packet to Host B (192.168.0.20). Describe every Layer 2 and Layer 3 event from Host A's perspective through the router to Host B.
choice_a: Host A sends a frame directly to Host B's MAC which the switch looks up and forwards across subnets without a router
choice_b: Host A sends an IP packet with Host B's MAC as destination; the router accepts it and replaces the source IP with its own
choice_c: Host A ARPs for gateway MAC; sends frame with router MAC and Host B's IP to router's local interface; router receives; strips L2; routes to 192.168.0.0/24; ARPs for Host B MAC on that subnet; sends new frame with router source MAC and Host B destination MAC carrying original IP packet with unchanged source/destination IPs
choice_d: Host A sends the packet to the DNS server which resolves 192.168.0.20 to Host B's MAC before forwarding to the router
explanation: Full path: (1) Host A needs to reach 192.168.0.20 — off-subnet so it sends to default gateway; (2) Host A ARPs for gateway IP on 10.0.100.0/24; gets router MAC; (3) Host A sends Ethernet frame: dst=router MAC; src=Host A MAC; IP dst=192.168.0.20; (4) router receives; strips Ethernet; makes IP route decision for 192.168.0.0/24 → exits on the 192.168.0.x interface; (5) router ARPs for Host B; gets Host B MAC; (6) router sends frame: dst=Host B MAC; src=router MAC (on this interface); IP src=10.0.100.5; IP dst=192.168.0.20 unchanged.
aspect_key: host arps gateway; sends to router mac; router re-encapsulates with destination host mac per-subnet
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Why does VTP actually increase operational risk for many enterprise networks even though it is designed to reduce administrative burden?
choice_a: VTP increases risk because it requires ISL trunking which is less stable than 802.1Q for VLAN propagation
choice_b: VTP increases risk because VLAN names in the VTP database are case-sensitive causing mismatches that drop all VLAN traffic
choice_c: VTP's revision number synchronization mechanism means that a single rogue or misconfigured switch with a higher revision number can instantaneously overwrite the VLAN database across the entire domain; this risk often outweighs the benefit of automated VLAN propagation in large production networks
choice_d: VTP increases risk because it requires all switches to be in VTP server mode simultaneously creating conflicting VLAN database versions
explanation: VTP revision wipeout risk: any switch that joins the VTP domain with a higher revision number (common on used equipment or test switches) automatically becomes authoritative. All VTP clients accept its database — even if that database deletes production VLANs. In large environments with hundreds of switches and thousands of users the risk of one such event causing a widespread outage is significant. Many enterprises disable VTP (transparent mode everywhere) and manage VLANs manually or via automation.
aspect_key: vtp revision wipeout risk often outweighs automation benefit in large production networks
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What happens when a switch receives a frame and the source MAC address is already in the MAC table but associated with a different port than the incoming frame's port?
choice_a: The switch drops the frame because MAC address conflicts are not permitted in the MAC table
choice_b: The switch updates the MAC table to associate the MAC address with the new ingress port overwriting the previous entry — this is normal behavior for mobile devices but could also indicate MAC spoofing or a topology change
choice_c: The switch sends an alert to the network management system and quarantines both ports until an administrator resolves the conflict
choice_d: The switch maintains both entries and uses round-robin forwarding for subsequent frames to that MAC
explanation: MAC address tables are not permanent. When a frame arrives with a source MAC that already exists in the table but on a different port the switch updates the entry to the new port. Legitimate reasons: device moved to a different switch port; laptop reconnected to different location. Suspicious reason: MAC spoofing or MAC flapping due to a loop. Rapid flapping of the same MAC across ports is a sign of a loop or attack.
aspect_key: switch updates mac table to new port when known mac arrives on different port
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: A switch has a 512 KB frame buffer. Sustained high-speed bursts from multiple ports destined for one slow port cause the buffer to fill. What happens and what design change addresses this?
choice_a: Frames are delayed proportionally and all ports slow to the speed of the bottleneck port automatically
choice_b: When the buffer fills new incoming frames for that destination port are dropped; design fix: increase the slow port's speed (upgrade to faster link) or implement QoS traffic shaping to prioritize critical traffic and control burst impact
choice_c: Buffer overflow redirects excess frames to the CPU for software forwarding which is slower but prevents drops
choice_d: The switch broadcasts overflow frames to all ports to prevent loss
explanation: Switch buffers are finite. When sustained input to a slow egress port exceeds buffer capacity new arriving frames are tail-dropped (discarded). Solutions: (1) faster egress port eliminates the bottleneck; (2) QoS queuing policies can prioritize higher-value traffic for the buffer space that remains; (3) traffic engineering to load-spread across multiple paths.
aspect_key: buffer overflow causes frame drops; fix by upgrading slow port speed or adding qos
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Describe the complete design of a small office network with three departments (Sales; IT; Finance) where each department must be isolated at Layer 2 but all must access the internet through one router.
choice_a: Sales and IT and Finance each get their own physical switch; the three switches connect to the router on separate ports; no trunking needed
choice_b: All three departments use one flat VLAN; NAT isolation at the router provides department separation
choice_c: Assign Sales to VLAN 10; IT to VLAN 20; Finance to VLAN 30 on a managed switch; configure a trunk link from the switch to the router; configure router subinterfaces (fa0/0.10; fa0/0.20; fa0/0.30) with encapsulation dot1Q and a unique gateway IP per VLAN; router NATs all three VLANs through one public IP to the internet
choice_d: Use one unmanaged switch with all ports in VLAN 1; configure ACLs on the router to simulate department separation
explanation: Design: (1) managed switch with VLANs 10; 20; 30; (2) assign Sales ports to VLAN 10; IT to VLAN 20; Finance to VLAN 30; (3) router-on-a-stick trunk from switch to router; (4) router subinterfaces for each VLAN with their respective gateway IPs and encapsulation dot1Q; (5) router applies NAT outbound to the internet. Layer 2 isolation by VLAN; Layer 3 routing by router; internet access by NAT.
aspect_key: vlan per department plus router-on-a-stick provides l2 isolation and shared internet via nat
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 16 - VLANs Made Easy/Transcript.md

question: What is the security argument for why Telnet should not be used even when the switch management VLAN is on a separate isolated VLAN?
choice_a: The management VLAN isolation prevents IP connectivity so Telnet cannot function anyway and SSH is required for the IP session to establish
choice_b: Telnet encryption works on management VLANs because the VLAN tag wraps the session in 802.1Q which provides confidentiality
choice_c: Even on a dedicated management VLAN a compromised host within that VLAN can capture all Telnet sessions since 802.1Q VLAN tags provide Layer 2 isolation not encryption; Telnet remains plaintext within the VLAN; any device on the management VLAN can read all credentials
choice_d: The management VLAN applies IP-layer encryption automatically so Telnet credentials are encrypted at the network layer
explanation: Management VLANs reduce the attack surface by limiting which devices can reach the management IP. But within the management VLAN traffic is still Ethernet — unencrypted. Any device that is in or gains access to the management VLAN can passively capture all Telnet sessions in cleartext. SSH encrypts the session so even a management VLAN sniffer cannot read credentials.
aspect_key: vlan isolation reduces attack surface but does not encrypt telnet; ssh required for credential protection
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 10 - Telnet and SSH/Transcript.md

question: A network contains switches A; B; and C connected in a line: A-B-C. Host 1 is on A; Host 2 is on C. Host 2's MAC is unknown to A and B. Host 1 sends a frame to Host 2. Trace the full event sequence including all MAC learning that occurs.
choice_a: Host 1 sends to switch A; A routes to C using IP addressing; C delivers to Host 2 directly
choice_b: Host 1 sends to A; A drops the frame because Host 2's MAC is unknown; Host 1 retransmits after ARP
choice_c: Host 1 sends a frame; A learns Host 1 MAC on its port; A floods to B and the link to A; B learns Host 1 MAC on the A-facing port; B floods to C and back to A; C learns Host 1 MAC on the B-facing port; C floods to Host 2 and other ports; Host 2 receives and replies; reply traverses C→B→A with MAC learning at each hop
choice_d: Host 1 sends a frame; A floods to all ports; switches B and C receive and drop it because Host 2's MAC is unknown
explanation: Detailed trace: (1) A receives frame from Host 1; learns Host 1 MAC on Host 1's port; (2) dst Host 2 unknown; A floods to B (and any other ports); (3) B receives tagged or untagged; learns Host 1 MAC on A-facing port; floods to C; (4) C receives; learns Host 1 MAC on B-facing port; floods to Host 2 and other ports; (5) Host 2 replies unicast to Host 1 MAC; (6) C learns Host 2 on Host 2's port; forwards to B; (7) B learns Host 2 on C-facing port; forwards to A; (8) A learns Host 2 on B-facing port; forwards to Host 1. All future traffic forwarded directly.
aspect_key: unknown unicast floods across all switches in chain; reply teaches all switches the mac locations
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Why does VLAN membership need to be intentionally manually created and assigned rather than auto-detected and what operational error does this prevent?
choice_a: VLANs cannot be auto-assigned because the VLAN ID field in the 802.1Q tag would have to be populated by the endpoint which requires a trunk NIC
choice_b: VLAN auto-detection is not supported because it would require ARP to carry VLAN metadata which is not part of the standard ARP packet format
choice_c: Intentional manual VLAN and port assignment ensures that a device plugging into an access port ends up in the intended broadcast domain and subnet; auto-detection could place a device in the wrong VLAN giving it unintended access to resources of a different department
choice_d: VLAN auto-assignment is technically supported but is disabled by default due to VMPS server licensing costs
explanation: Manual VLAN assignment is a security and design control. If port VLAN assignment were automatic a device plugging into any port could end up in any VLAN — potentially one it should not access. By manually assigning each access port to a specific VLAN the administrator controls exactly which broadcast domain each physical connection lands in. This is fundamental to the security model of VLAN segmentation.
aspect_key: manual vlan assignment prevents devices from accidentally or maliciously landing in wrong vlans
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: An ISP provides a single public IP address. An internal network has 50 hosts each with a private IP in 192.168.1.0/24. All 50 attempt simultaneous TCP connections to an external web server at 203.0.113.1 port 80. How does NAT handle this without confusing which replies go where?
choice_a: NAT assigns each host a temporary unique public IP from the ISP's pool during the session
choice_b: NAT drops connections beyond the first one because only one host can use the public IP at a time
choice_c: NAT blocks the 50 TCP connections and forces hosts to queue and connect sequentially using the same source port
choice_d: NAT translates each internal host's IP and unique TCP source port to the same public IP but a unique translated source port; the session table maps each combination back to the correct private host
explanation: PAT (Port Address Translation) — a form of NAT: each of the 50 hosts has a unique private IP and TCP source port. The router translates all to the public IP but assigns each a unique translated port number. Session table entry: (private 192.168.1.x:src-port ↔ public IP:translated-port ↔ server 203.0.113.1:80). When replies arrive the translated port identifies which private host gets the reply.
aspect_key: pat uses unique translated ports to multiplex 50 simultaneous sessions through one public ip
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What is the full sequence of problems that occur when broadcast traffic grows too large on a flat switched network and how does VLAN segmentation specifically address each problem?
choice_a: Broadcast traffic growth only affects hub-based networks; switches are immune because they forward broadcasts to the specific destination port
choice_b: VLAN segmentation addresses broadcast storms by enabling STP which blocks loops that generate excess broadcasts
choice_c: Broadcast growth: (1) consumes increasing bandwidth leaving less for actual user traffic; (2) each host CPU must process every broadcast interrupt; (3) on a hub all broadcasts are also a sniffing opportunity; VLAN segmentation confines broadcasts to one VLAN so only the relevant hosts process them; bandwidth consumption is isolated per VLAN; and sniffing access is limited to one VLAN's members
choice_d: VLAN segmentation reduces broadcasts by using unicast ARP replies so hosts can cache MAC addresses without repeated broadcasts
explanation: Without VLANs broadcasts hit all hosts: (1) bandwidth: every ARP; DHCP; NetBIOS; OSPF hello etc. consumes trunk and access port bandwidth; (2) CPU: each host NIC passes the frame to the OS for processing even if the app ignores it; (3) security: any host can observe all broadcast traffic. VLANs confine each broadcast to its domain — a VLAN with 30 hosts only exposes those 30 hosts to that VLAN's broadcasts. Other VLANs are unaffected.
aspect_key: vlans confine broadcasts reducing bandwidth consumption cpu load and broadcast sniffing
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 16 - VLANs Made Easy/Transcript.md

question: What is the distinction between 802.1Q's role in the forwarding plane versus VTP's role in the control plane and why can't VTP replace 802.1Q?
choice_a: VTP can replace 802.1Q if the switch is in VTP server mode because the server controls both VLAN tagging and database propagation
choice_b: VTP and 802.1Q are redundant; both tag frames identically; the administrator chooses one per deployment
choice_c: 802.1Q operates in the forwarding plane — it defines how individual frames are tagged with VLAN IDs as they traverse trunk links; VTP operates in the control plane — it defines how VLAN metadata (IDs and names) is propagated between switches; VTP describes what VLANs exist; 802.1Q carries their traffic
choice_d: VTP replaces 802.1Q in VTP server mode because the server provides the tagging function across the domain
explanation: Planes of operation: 802.1Q is data-plane — it marks every frame with a VLAN ID so switches know which VLAN to deliver it to. VTP is control-plane — it synchronizes the VLAN database (what VLANs exist by ID and name) across switches. Without 802.1Q VLAN-tagged traffic cannot traverse trunks. Without VTP admins configure each switch's VLAN table manually. Both are needed for different functions.
aspect_key: 802.1q tags frames in the data plane; vtp synchronizes vlan database in the control plane
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 17 - Trunking and 802.1Q/Transcript.md

question: Explain why MAC address transparency is both a feature and a limitation of Layer 2 switches and when this limitation requires a Layer 3 solution.
choice_a: Transparency is a limitation only on hubs; switches rewrite MAC addresses at each hop making them non-transparent by design
choice_b: Transparency allows switches to forward frames without modifying them making them invisible in the path; this is a feature for simplicity but a limitation because MAC addresses have no notion of logical network organization; once two hosts are on different subnets Layer 2 alone cannot determine which physical segment to use — Layer 3 IP routing is needed to bridge logical network boundaries
choice_c: Transparency is a security risk because the switch cannot inspect IP addresses to block inter-VLAN traffic; a Layer 3 switch adds IP inspection to fill this gap
choice_d: Transparency is only relevant for IEEE 802.3 Ethernet; VLANs eliminate transparency by adding VLAN IDs to each frame
explanation: MAC transparency: switches forward frames without altering them — endpoints are unaware a switch handled their frame. Benefit: simplicity and speed; no need to manage per-switch MAC assignments. Limitation: MAC addresses are flat identifiers with no hierarchical structure. IP subnets impose logical structure on a network — but MAC forwarding cannot navigate across subnet boundaries. Once the destination is in a different subnet a Layer 3 device is needed to make the forwarding decision across network boundaries.
aspect_key: mac transparency is simple but flat; ip routing is needed for cross-subnet logical addressing
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: A trunk link between two switches allows VLANs 1-100. An administrator creates VLAN 101 on SW1 but the VLAN is not in VTP (switches are in transparent mode). What happens to traffic tagged with VLAN 101 arriving at SW2 from SW1 and what is required to fix it?
choice_a: SW2 accepts VLAN 101 because trunks carry all VLANs by default up to 4094 regardless of local VLAN database
choice_b: SW2 will only drop frames for VLANs above 100 because 802.1Q is limited to 100 VLANs
choice_c: SW2 drops VLAN 101 frames because VLAN 101 does not exist in SW2's VLAN database; fix: manually create VLAN 101 on SW2 (and also allow VLAN 101 on the trunk if it was explicitly pruned from the allowed list)
choice_d: SW2 automatically creates VLAN 101 dynamically when it receives the first tagged frame for it
explanation: Two problems: (1) VTP transparent mode means each switch manages its own VLAN database independently — VLAN 101 must be created manually on SW2; (2) the trunk's allowed VLAN list must include 101 (by default all VLANs are allowed but explicit pruning could have excluded 101). Fix: vlan 101 on SW2 + verify allowed VLAN list on the trunk.
aspect_key: vtp transparent mode requires manual vlan creation on each switch; no automatic propagation
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: What are the implications of an advanced Layer 3 switch versus a standard Layer 2 switch in the context of VLAN inter-routing and how does this remove the router-on-a-stick requirement?
choice_a: A Layer 3 switch cannot do inter-VLAN routing; it only adds OSPF support to the Layer 2 switch feature set
choice_b: Layer 3 switches require separate physical interfaces for each VLAN just like a router-on-a-stick configuration
