choice_c: A Layer 3 switch has routing hardware built in; it can perform inter-VLAN routing internally using SVI interfaces with IP addresses per VLAN; traffic routes at line rate in hardware without needing an external router; this eliminates router-on-a-stick bandwidth bottleneck and latency
choice_d: Layer 3 switches replace the MAC address table with an IP routing table; MAC-based forwarding is no longer used
explanation: Layer 3 (multilayer) switch: it has both MAC-based Layer 2 switching and IP-based Layer 3 routing in hardware. Each VLAN gets an SVI with an IP address. Inter-VLAN routing is performed in hardware at line rate — no external router required. This eliminates the router-on-a-stick bottleneck and is the standard design in modern enterprise access/distribution layers.
aspect_key: layer 3 switch routes between vlans internally via svIs eliminating router-on-a-stick
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: A company uses site-to-site VPN to connect headquarters and a branch office. A branch employee's workstation (branch LAN IP 10.1.0.50) opens a session to an internal server at headquarters (HQ LAN IP 10.2.0.100). Trace every network layer event from the branch workstation to the server.
choice_a: Branch workstation sends packet to its default gateway; gateway applies NAT changing source to public IP; HQ server receives from public IP; no VPN involved
choice_b: Branch workstation sends packet; branch router checks routing table; matches VPN policy (10.1.0.0/24 ↔ 10.2.0.0/24); encrypts packet in VPN tunnel; sends encrypted packet across internet to HQ VPN gateway; HQ gateway decrypts; routes original packet to server at 10.2.0.100
choice_c: Branch workstation ARPs for 10.2.0.100 across the VPN; HQ router replies with server MAC; workstation sends frame directly
choice_d: Branch workstation sends packet to DNS which resolves 10.2.0.100 to a routable public IP before the VPN can be applied
explanation: Site-to-site VPN path: (1) workstation sends IP packet (src 10.1.0.50; dst 10.2.0.100) to branch default gateway; (2) branch router matches VPN policy: destination 10.2.0.0/24 goes through tunnel; (3) router encrypts packet; wraps in VPN headers addressed to HQ gateway's public IP; (4) encrypted packet crosses internet; routers forward by outer IP headers; (5) HQ VPN gateway receives; decrypts; strips tunnel headers; (6) original packet (src 10.1.0.50; dst 10.2.0.100) is routed to server; (7) server replies through the same tunnel in reverse.
aspect_key: site-to-site vpn encrypts packet in tunnel for internet transit then decrypts at hq gateway
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Compare the broadcast domain impact of a flat 100-host network versus a segmented 4-VLAN network with 25 hosts each and calculate how many devices each ARP request reaches in each scenario.
choice_a: Flat: 100 devices; each ARP reaches 25 hosts on average because switches selectively forward ARP. 4-VLAN: each ARP reaches all 100 hosts because VLAN trunks extend broadcasts
choice_b: Flat: ARP reaches all 100 hosts. 4-VLAN: ARP reaches only the 24 other hosts in the same VLAN (25 total including sender); hosts in other VLANs receive nothing; segmentation reduces ARP scope by 75%
choice_c: Flat: ARP reaches 50 hosts on average due to STP blocking half the ports. 4-VLAN: ARP reaches 12 hosts because VLANs use unicast ARP
choice_d: Flat and 4-VLAN have the same ARP scope because routers forward ARP to all subnets
explanation: Flat network (1 broadcast domain): any ARP request reaches all 99 other hosts. 4-VLAN network (4 broadcast domains of 25 each): any ARP request reaches only the 24 other hosts in the same VLAN. Hosts in other VLANs receive nothing — the broadcast is contained. This reduces per-device broadcast processing by 75% and reduces the bandwidth consumed by broadcasts by 75% within each VLAN segment.
aspect_key: 4-vlan segmentation reduces arp broadcast reach from 99 to 24 hosts a 75 percent reduction
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 16 - VLANs Made Easy/Transcript.md

question: What are the three Telnet operational uses and how does each differ in risk profile compared to using SSH?
choice_a: Telnet for remote management has low risk because switches reject automated scanning tools; Telnet for port testing is medium risk; Telnet for server management is high risk only on internet-facing hosts
choice_b: All three Telnet uses carry the same risk level; SSH and Telnet are equivalent in risk within a trusted network
choice_c: Remote server management over Telnet: high risk — any intercepted session reveals full admin credentials and commands. Network device management over Telnet: high risk — full switch/router config exposure. Port connectivity testing (telnet host port): lower risk — used to confirm TCP port is open; sends no credentials; but reveals network connectivity topology to anyone monitoring
choice_d: Telnet port testing is illegal in most jurisdictions; only remote management uses are permissible for network administrators
explanation: Risk breakdown: (1) Remote management of servers — full session capture gives root/admin access to servers; (2) Network device management — full session gives full network control including VLAN and routing config; (3) Port testing (telnet IP port) — no authentication; just tests if TCP session establishes; lower risk but still reveals which ports are open to network monitors.
aspect_key: three telnet uses differ in risk: management is high risk; port testing sends no credentials
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 10 - Telnet and SSH/Transcript.md

question: Why does the statement that switches solve the hub's performance and security problems require a nuance when VLANs are considered?
choice_a: Switches do not solve performance problems because they still use CSMA/CD on inter-switch trunk links
choice_b: Switches solve the collision problem completely but still place all ports in one broadcast domain by default; without VLAN segmentation the security isolation benefit is incomplete and broadcasts still reach all hosts; VLANs are required to fully realize the security and performance benefits of switching
choice_c: Switches solve both problems completely by default without VLANs; VLANs are only needed for advanced multi-tenant deployments
choice_d: Switches increase the security problem by adding 802.1Q tagging overhead that is exploitable for VLAN hopping on all networks
explanation: Switches solve collisions (separate collision domains) and targeted delivery (unicast-only frames). But by default all ports are in VLAN 1 — one large broadcast domain. ARP; DHCP; and other broadcasts still reach all hosts. A device in promiscuous mode still sees all broadcasts and flooded frames. VLANs are required to segment the broadcast domain and limit Layer 2 visibility across departments.
aspect_key: switches solve collision and delivery targeting; vlans additionally required to segment broadcasts
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: How does the managed switch web interface requirement relate to the VLAN-capable switch requirement for Week 8 lab work?
choice_a: Web interface and VLAN support are independent features; a switch can support VLANs without a web interface if it has a CLI
choice_b: Managed switches with web interfaces are only required for VLAN configuration on Cisco equipment; other vendors use CLI-only managed switches
choice_c: Both requirements converge: VLAN configuration requires a managed switch (has configuration interfaces — CLI; web; or both); a web interface is one form of management interface; an unmanaged switch has neither a web interface nor VLAN support
choice_d: A web interface is required specifically for 802.1Q VLAN tagging because the GUI constructs the 802.1Q tag fields; CLI cannot configure 802.1Q
explanation: The convergence: (1) VLANs require configuration — port assignments; VLAN IDs; trunk setup; (2) configuration requires a management interface — CLI or web; (3) unmanaged switches have no management interface at all; (4) a managed switch satisfies both requirements — it has a configurable management interface and supports VLAN features. Web interface is just one type of management interface alongside CLI.
aspect_key: vlan configuration requires managed switch which has the cli or web interface for configuration

question: A network engineer is designing a new office. They want to: (a) support 4 departments on one switch; (b) allow inter-department communication through a firewall; (c) back up all switch configs nightly. Which combination of technologies addresses all three requirements?
choice_a: 4 VLANs on a managed switch; VLANs route traffic to the firewall which inspects and permits inter-department traffic; TFTP with a scheduled copy running-config tftp job backs up switch configs nightly
choice_b: One flat VLAN with firewall ACLs for department isolation; manual config backup by administrators weekly
choice_c: 4 VLANs on a managed switch; router-on-a-stick for direct inter-VLAN routing without firewall inspection; NetFlow for config backup
choice_d: 4 separate switches (one per department) with a dedicated firewall connection per switch; configs backed up to a shared drive
explanation: Design mapping: (a) 4 VLANs on one managed switch — satisfies department isolation requirement; (b) router/firewall as the inter-VLAN routing device — all traffic between departments is inspected by the firewall before being forwarded; (c) TFTP server with copy running-config tftp scheduled command — saves nightly config backups. All three requirements addressed with standard technologies covered in the course.
aspect_key: vlans provide isolation; firewall handles inter-vlan routing; tftp backs up switch configs

question: How does the concept of virtual not physical VLAN membership enable more flexible network design than physically separate switches?
choice_a: VLANs are physically not virtually separated; the virtual aspect refers only to the VLAN ID numbering which is a software concept
choice_b: Virtual VLAN membership allows the same broadcast domain to span wireless and wired infrastructure automatically
choice_c: Because VLAN membership is a logical configuration of a switch port rather than a physical cable connection — a device's VLAN can be changed by reconfiguring the port; departments can be reorganized; devices can be moved; and new VLANs can be created without any physical cabling changes
choice_d: Virtual VLAN membership is only flexible when combined with dynamic VLAN assignment; static port-based VLANs have the same inflexibility as physical switches
explanation: Physical separation: moving a device to a different department means physically moving it to a different switch or rewiring. Virtual VLAN: moving the same device means issuing one command (switchport access vlan <new-id>) on the switch. No cable changes; no hardware additions. This makes network reorganization fast and cheap — a major operational advantage of VLAN design.
aspect_key: vlan membership is logical so departments can be reorganized with commands not cable moves

question: What is the interaction between the switch MAC table aging timer and the flooding behavior and what network problem can expired entries cause?
choice_a: Expired MAC table entries cause the switch to drop frames permanently for devices that have not transmitted recently
choice_b: MAC table aging only applies to trunk ports; access port MAC entries never expire
choice_c: When a MAC table entry ages out the switch no longer knows which port to use for that destination; the next frame for that MAC causes a flood; if many entries expire simultaneously (e.g. after a switch reboot) a burst of flooding occurs temporarily increasing broadcast-like load; this resolves as devices retransmit and entries are relearned
choice_d: MAC table aging causes the switch to revert to hub behavior permanently until an administrator runs clear mac address-table
explanation: MAC table entries have an aging timer (typically 300 seconds default). When a device stops transmitting its entry ages out. If another device then sends to that MAC the switch floods — identical to the initial unknown unicast scenario. After a reboot or clear command all entries are lost and a flood burst occurs as all devices rediscover each other. This is transient; it resolves as traffic flows and entries are relearned.
aspect_key: expired mac entries trigger flooding; reboot causes burst flood that resolves as entries relearn
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Textbook 1 - Chapter 3 Switches

question: Why is the combination of 802.1Q VLAN tagging on trunk links and hard-coded access/trunk port modes the complete solution to VLAN hopping prevention; and why is either element alone insufficient?
choice_a: 802.1Q tagging alone prevents VLAN hopping because the tag limits which VLANs each frame can access
choice_b: Hard-coded port modes alone prevent VLAN hopping even without 802.1Q because access mode strips all tags before forwarding
choice_c: Hard-coded access mode prevents DTP trunk negotiation eliminating the attack vector where a rogue device negotiates a trunk; but 802.1Q is still needed on the legitimate trunks to correctly mark traffic by VLAN; without 802.1Q all inter-switch traffic would be untagged and fall into the native VLAN. Neither 802.1Q alone (DTP auto still allows trunk negotiation) nor hard-coded mode alone (without 802.1Q trunks cannot carry multiple VLANs) is sufficient — both together form the complete design
choice_d: 802.1Q tagging and hard-coded mode are redundant; either alone provides complete VLAN hopping protection
explanation: Two-layer protection: (1) Hard-coded access mode on user ports — disables DTP; rogue devices cannot negotiate trunks; eliminates the VLAN hopping attack vector. (2) 802.1Q on trunk ports — correctly identifies which VLAN each frame belongs to as it crosses inter-switch links; without it traffic cannot be segregated by VLAN on trunks. Access mode alone doesn't configure trunks. 802.1Q alone doesn't stop DTP negotiation on access ports. Both required together.
aspect_key: access mode stops dtp negotiation; 802.1q enables vlan-tagged trunks; both required together
